title: Blanche Catherine Medhurst
text:
(London 1848- Barnstaple 1925)
social worker and contributor to MP (1873-5)
She was daughter of Francis Hastings Medhurst (d.1852), of Kippax Hall, near Leeds, and Mary Anne Bushnan; a friend of Mrs Mercier and patroness of Emmeline Spratt. She published a study of workhouses, The Idle Room (1886). [LG 1871, census] The 1861 census shows Mary Anne Medhurst with two other daughters and her mother Anne Bushman (d.1878) living at 29 Dorset Square; in the 1871 census Blanche is there too. Hers is an odd story, because her father killed another boy while a private pupil at school and got three years for manslaughter in 1839, coming up before Mr Justice Coleridge; his grandfather, a rich stockbroker, had murdered his wife while insane and was at that time in an asylum. [Times]. She wrote an article in MP (1886) about the Associated Workers League, of which she was evidently a member.

title: Mary Sophia Paulina Martyn
text:
(1843-1887)
member of the Gosling Society
A Gosling as ‘Pena’, 1859-63, ‘Pena’ was her nickname in real life as well. She was the only child of the Rev. Thomas Waddon Martyn (1808-1846), Rector of Lifton and Vicar of Thorverton, and his wife Sophia (1815/6-1898), daughter of the Rev. Dr. James Coleridge (1789-1857) and his wife Sophia, daughter of Col. Stanhope Badcock. In the 1861 census she was living with this grandmother in Dawlish. She was a cousin of CMY’s, and also of Bishop Patteson's, and he seems to have made a pet of her before his departure for New Zealand, mentioning her in his letters. [Vivian, Visitations of Cornwall;  1851 census;  PO Devon 1866;  1881 census with mother at Harleston, Cary Crescent, Torquay]

text:

[LG 1906 Baker of Bayfordbury, 1871 census]

title: Mary Ann Marriott
text:
(b.Bradfield 1845)
younger daughter of the Rev. John Marriott
Either she or her elder sister Maria was 'Money Spinner', a particularly brilliant contributor to the Spider Society in the Monthly Packet. In the 1901 census they were living together at Eastleigh, and one of them must therefore be the Miss Marriott who organised the reprinting of Last Heartsease Leaves in 1900.
In the 1891 census her cousin Susanna Judith Watson and visitor Amy Marriott Watson are present (the two latter appear as daughters of the Rev. John Watson, Vicar of Guilsborough, Northants in the 1861 census). Was there a relationship to H. B. Marriott Watson? [1851 census; birth index; 1881 census Eastleigh; 1891, 1901]

title: Miss Marriott
text:
contributor to MP (1874-85) as Money Spinner
Money Spinner was either Maria Marriott or Mary Ann Marriott, of Hythe, who was one of the most effective Spiders, according to CMY; they were daughters of the Rev. John Marriott, Vicar of St. John the Baptist, Hythe, in the New Forest. The family later moved to Eastleigh, where the two daughters did good works. [Moor, Erminois]

title: Amy Aubrey Locke
text:
(1881-1916)
historian
First holder of the Charlotte Yonge scholarship from Winchester High School, she was daughter of Alfred L. Locke, head porter to Winchester College. She is listed as a student at Somerville College Oxford in the 1901 census. She published several works of history before her early death and was employed by the Central Office of the Victoria County History project. She seems to have called herself Audrey in adult life.
For a description of how her substantial contribution to the Cornwall section Victoria History of the Counties of England remained unpublished owing to the dilatoriness of another contributor, see John Beckett, [[otherbook:1116]'Canon Thomas Taylor of St. Just and the Victoria County History in Cornwall, 1899-1938'] Journal of the Royal Institution of Cornwall (2011) 31-44, 38-9. Probably more material on her professional career is to be found in the Victoria County History Archive, held by the Institute of Historical Research, University of London.

title: Amy Aubrey Locke

(1881-1916)
historian
First holder of the Charlotte Yonge scholarship from Winchester High School, she was daughter of Alfred L. Locke, head porter to Winchester College. She is listed as a student at Somerville College Oxford in the 1901 census. She published several works of history before her early death and was employed by the Central Office of the Victoria County History project. She seems to have called herself Audrey in adult life.
For a description of how her substantial contribution to the Cornwall section Victoria History of the Counties of England remained unpublished owing to the dilatoriness of another contributor, see John Beckett, [[otherbook:1116]'Canon Thomas Taylor of St. Just and the Victoria County History in Cornwall, 1899-1938'] Journal of the Royal Institution of Cornwall (2011) 31-44, 38-9. text:
of Philadelphia

title: Henrietta Louisa (Farrer) Lear
text:
(1824-1896)
author
She was the widow of the Rev. Sidney Lear (d.1867), and was living at 15 The Close, Salisbury in the 1891 census. She published devotional and historical works and fiction from 1835, mainly anonymously but sometimes as ‘Mrs Sidney Lear’. Henry James, reviewing her 1875 life of Hyppolite Flandrin in the Nation (26 August 1875), described the tone as ‘a trifle “goody” perhaps - a trifle too suggestive of what is called “Sunday reading”’.

title: The Rt.Rev. William Awdry
text:
(14 Jan 1842-4 Jan 1910)
Vicar of Amport, later Bishop of Osaka
Successively Vicar of Amport, suffragan bishop of Southampton (1895), Bishop of Osaka (1896-8) and South Tokyo (1898-1908), he married (4 August 1868) (Frances) Emily Moberly. Son of Sir John Wither Awdry (1795-1878), chief justice of Bombay, he was brother of Frances Awdry, Charles Awdry and the Rev. Vere Awdry. His brother Charles Awdry married Margaret Helen Moberly. Sir John Awdry’s mother Jane Bigg was the sister of Sir William Heathcote’s mother Elizabeth (Bigg) Heathcote, and he was therefore cousin to Sir William and to the Rev. William Bigg Wither. [WWW]

title: Mary Elizabeth (Wormeley) Latimer
text:
(1822-1904)
American historian, contributor to MP (1879-93)
She lived in Baltimore, Maryland, and published two novels as Elizabeth Wormeley: Amabel: A Family History (1853) and Our Cousin Veronica, or, Scenes and Adventures Over the Blue Ridge (1855). In the 1880s she began writing again, publishing three novels under the name Elizabeth Wormeley Latimer: Salvage (1880), My Wife and My Wife's Sister, 1881, and Princess Amélie: A Fragment of Autobiography (1883). In 1888 she published three translations from the French: History of the People of Israel, by Ernest Renan, and For Fifteen Years and Steel Hammer, both by Louis Ulbach and in 1890 George Sand’s Nanon. In the 1890s and early 1900s she published a series of books on the history of various European nations, and on the colonisation of Africa, three more translations and a final novel, Prince Incognito (1902).

title: Charlotte (Warren) Latham
text:
(b.Sussex 1801/2)
mother of Margaret Roberts
According to George Ormerod, Parentalia (privately printed 1851), she was the daughter of the Rev. Henry Warren, prebendary of Bangor. She married, first, the Rev. Ellis Roberts, Vicar of Llanynys, and, secondly (1848), as his second wife, the Rev. Henry Latham. She was living with the latter at Fittleworth in the 1851 census; at Tormoham, Torquay in 1871 with her stepdaughter Dina [sic] Latham, daughter Margaret Roberts, and stepgranddaughters Margaret and Katharine Mary Hutchingstone, 11 and 7;  in the 1881 census again at Tormoham with Diana Latham and Margaret Roberts and granddaughter Helen M Latham, 13.
See under Grace Latham for the implication that there was a blood relationship between either Charlotte Latham or her first husband and some members of the Latham family.

title: Edith Emma (Butler) Knight
text:
(4? March 1851-1936)
member of the Gosling Society, daughter of the Very Rev. William John Butler
Known as Emma Butler, she was the daughter of CMY’s great friends the Rev. William Butler, Vicar of Wantage and later Dean of Lincoln and his wife Emma Barnett (1813-1894). A member of the Gosling Society as ‘Frog’, she married (Oct-Dec 1877) as his third wife, Edward Lewis Knight (1817-1882), of Hornacott Manor, Cornwall.  She was an associate of the Wantage sisterhood.  She inherited her aunt Elizabeth Barnett’s letters from CMY and lent them to both Coleridge and Romanes. [LG 1906 Knight of Wolverley, will, Life and Letters of Dean Butler; Memoirs of Arthur John Butler]

She was also presumably editor or author of the other works appearing under the name ‘Eadgyth’, which include a contribution to the Christmas number of MP (1890). Perhaps she was the Miss E. Jupp who was paid £3 on 20 June 1872. Perhaps Edith Margaret Jupp (b. 1847/8), daughter of Edward Basil Jupp (1812-1877), solicitor and antiquary of Greenwich. [1871 census, CMY bank account, ODNB E.B.Jupp]

title: Ellen (Rice) Joyce The Hon. Mrs
text:
(1832-1924)
charity organizer
Daughter of the 5th Lord Dynevor, wife from 1855of the Rev. James Gerald Joyce (1819-1878), Rector of Stratfield Saye, she was a founding associate of the GFS, and president of the United British Women’s Emigration Association, 1888. She was Hon. Sec. of the GFS Emigration Department and was awarded the CBE in 1920. She lived with her son the Rev. Arthur Joyce (b. 1856), Rector of Winnall, at St. John’s Croft, Winchester, from 1887. [ODNB, PB]

title: Rowland Jones-Bateman
text:
(1826-16 Dec 1896)
of Otterbourne Grange, Eastleigh
His name is sometimes spelt Jones Bateman. He was a barrister, educated at Winchester and New College, Oxford. He made friends with JBY on fishing trip to Norway, hence moved to Otterbourne, conspicuous in parish work.  His wife was Jessy Burlton (b. 1839/40). The family were in London in the 1871, Otterbourne in the 1881 census. [CMY’s obituary in the Otterbourne Parish Magazine; LG 1952 Jones Bateman]

title: Charles Henry Johns
text:
(1844-1915)
son of the Rev. Charles Alexander Johns
Eldest son of Charles Alexander Johns, he was educated at Rugby and Oriel College, Oxford, and taught at Winton House until after his father’s death, later at other schools. In 1886 married Mary Emily Chalk who ran a school for girls at Lydney which they moved to Hendon. He is mentioned in his mother’s will, along with his sister Katie, as having brought her to poverty.[Alumni Oxonienses, information from Melissa Hardie]

title: The Rev. Charles Alexander Johns
text:
(1811-1874)
naturalist
He taught at Helston Grammar School, when Derwent Coleridge was headmaster, and later ran a prep school at Winton House, Winchester. He was founder and president of the Winchester Literary and Scientific Society. He married (1843) Ellen Julia Field (b. Bath 1821/2) Beenham in 1851 census, Watford in 1861. His father was a Plymouth banker, whose firm had collapsed in 1825. His most famous book was the very popular Flowers of the Field (1851). [ODNB]

title: The Rev. Canon George Edward Jelf
text:
(29 Jan1834 -19 Nov 1908)
Vicar of Saffron Walden
He married (1861) Fanny Crawley (d. 1865); three of their four children died in 1871. He married secondly (20 Jan 1876), Katharine Frances Dalton, and had four more children.  He was a canon of Rochester in 1880 and of St. Albans in 1878, master of the Charterhouse in 1907.  He was son of R. W. Jelf, principal of KCL 1844-69 and a friend of Keble and Pusey. His first wife’s biography is in G.E.Jelf A Memoir.

title: Gertrude Louisa Le Grand Jacob
text:
(b.1837?)
contributor to MP (1873)
Her contribution is signed ‘G. L. J.’. She was sister of Edith and Edgar Jacob. Their father’s youngest brother, Sir George Le Grand Jacob (1805-1881), retired after a distinguished career in the Indian army in 1861, and took Gertrude to live with him as his adopted daughter; she collaborated on some of his oriental researches.

title: The Rt. Rev. Edgar Jacob
text:
(1844-1920)
Bishop of Newcastle, then St. Albans
Educated at New Coll, Oxford,  he was Chaplain to Bishop of Calcutta 1872-6, vicar of Portsea from 1878, hon. canon of Winchester 1884, Bishop of Newcastle 1895, then St. Albans 1903. He was third son of the Rev. Dr. Philip Jacob of Crawley, Hants and Anna Sophia Noel.  He never married. His sister Edith Jacob founded the Society of Watchers and Workers. [Alumni Oxonienses;  Clergy List]

title: Arthur Donald Innes
text:
(Simla 1863/4-1938)
editor and publisher
He graduated from Oriel College Oxford in 1887, was publisher of MP and of some of CMY’s books, contributor (1892-8), took over from CMY as coeditor of MP in 1894. After the collapse of his publishing business, he seems to have worked for Cassell’s and later lectured in Indian history at SOAS. He came from a family of Indian administrators and civil servants. [WWW]

title: The Rev. Lewis Macnaughton Humbert
text:
(1819–1896)
Master of St. Cross Hospital
He was successively Master of St. Cross Hospital (1855-68) and Rector of Chiddingfold; he was later (1878) Vicar of St. Bartholomew Hyde, Winchester. He married (1850) Ann Parker (b. 1823/4) and had children Ellen (b. 1850/1), Francis, Lewis (b. 1856/7) and Margaret (b. 1858/9), who married the Rev. Alfred R. Wiseman. He was brother of the architect Albert Jenkins Humbert (1821–1877). [ODNB his brother; census; Alumni Oxonienses]

title: Mary Emily Hullah
text:
(b. 1847/8)
novelist, contributor to MP (1891)
She was daughter of the musicologist John Pyke Hullah (1812-1884), a friend of Thomas Helmore and Derwent Coleridge; in the 1881 census she was in London with her brother Francis, clerk to Lord Chief Justice Coleridge, and working as a governess; in the 1891 census she was staying with George Richmond the painter and described herself as an authoress. She probably also contributed to the Barnacle.

title: The Rev. William George Henderson
text:
(1819–1905)
contributor to MP (1862)
He married (1852) Jane Melville (d. 1901); he was contributor to MP (1862) as W. H.; he may also be the contributor of 1866 with the same signature. He was one of the editors of the Surtees Society’s publications, and was evidently the author of the series of articles ‘Notes on the Folk Lore of the North of England’ which appeared in the Monthly Packet in 1862 signed ‘W. H.’. His ODNB entry implies that he did not move from Jersey to become headmaster of Leeds Grammar School until 1862, but this letter suggests that he was already residing in the north of England in the previous year.

schoolmaster, contributor to MP (1885), publisher
Son of Sir William Heathcote, he married (20 Aug 1879) Mary Forbes Young (d. 13 Jan 1903), daughter of the Rev. James Gavin Young, Vicar of Hursley. For some years he ran a prep school at Hursley. However, he is listed in the 1891 census as a 'publisher', and it seems likely that he was the son of Sir William whom CMY mentioned in 1887 as working for George Allen and Sons.

title: Mary Arthur
text:
missionary
Mentioned, as a missionary who came to South Africa in 1861-2 and looked after orphans, by A.E.M. Anderson Morshead, A Pioneer and Founder: Reminiscences of Some who Knew Robert Gray D D, First Bishop of Cape Town (London: Skeffington 1905), and presumably the Miss Arthur referred to by CMY (6 Dec 1866). See 'The Ladies' Association, or, Great Causes Helped by Small Means' MP (May 1867) 510: 'Little native girls may also be maintained in such boarding-schools as the Missions possess. Such may be found at Miss Arthur's, at the Cape, where the cost is £6 per annum'.
See the website of the institution today at http://www.st-georges.org.za/about.html

title: Evelyn Gee
text:

contributor to MP (1872)
CMY’s bank account records the payment of £5 to Miss E. Gee on 10 April 1873, the only one to a person of this name. A story ‘What the Church Eagle Said’ appeared in MP (December 1872) 4. Evelyn Gee (b. Herts. 1843/4), daughter of the Rev. Richard Gee, Vicar of Abbots Langley and later of Windsor, who married (1880) Richard Rivington Holmes, librarian at Windsor Castle, seems a plausible candidate. He has an entry in ODNB, which states that she survived him.

He was probably ‘Vertumnus II’, dating from Bitton Vicarage, Bristol, who took over conducting the ’Monthly Packet Botanical Society’in the February 1884 issue, and ran it at least until January 1886. His predecessor, who started the society in the June 1879 issue and dated from Preston Vicarage, Sandwich, was perhaps the Rt. Rev. Henry Lascelles Jenner or a member of his family.

title: Louisa Dundas
text:
contributor to MP (1890, and probably also 1887)
She contributed articles on the Cambridge Local Examination, and perhaps also the other articles signed 'L. D.' (1875-89). From the obituary 'L. Dundas' contributed to MP (1887) (2) 151, of her youngest sister Adela Dundas (1840-1887) it is clear that she cannot be Katharine Louisa Dundas (b. 1849), youngest sister of Mary Frances Dundas. It is also clear from this source that her father died in 1883. She was probably the Louisa Dundas christened in February 1834, daughter of William Pitt Dundas (d.1883), Registrar General of Scotland, and his cousin and wife Mary Strange, whose daughter Adela was christened 1840 (there were several other children too). He was a member of the family of Dundas of Arniston.

title: The Rev. Edmund Doidge Anderson Morshead
text:
(1849-1912)
cousin of CMY, schoolmaster
Third son of Rev. John Philip Anderson Morshead and Alethea Yonge, he was a brilliant child and educated at Winchester and New College, Oxford 1867-72. He got a double first and returned to serve as assistant master at Winchester College 1872-1904. He married (1 Aug 1878) Mary, fourth sister of William Andrewes Fearon, headmaster of Winchester College 1884-91. He died of General Paralysis of the Insane after having spent some months in Holloway Sanatorium [Winchester College Register;  Wainewright, Winchester College; ODNB; LG 1871 Morshead; Hants RO MS 67M922W/82/21]

title: Elizabeth Charlotte Curteis
text:
(b.1845)
contributor to MP
This person was author, as Bessie C. Curteis, of In the Marsh (SPCK 1876). East Sussex Record Office MS AMS6128/3/27 is a letter (June 1875) from Bessie Charlotte Curteis to her father Edward Barrett Curteis of Leasam House, Rye, Sussex, stating that she has been offered £50 for the manuscript of her article in the Monthly Packet. She was no doubt the 'B.C.C.' who wrote 'On Dialect' MP (February 1874) 171-184.
It therefore seems reasonable to assume that she is the 'Miss Curteis' of Leasams to whom CMY was writing about 'your eagle and goose story', in a letter probably dating from 1872.
Her mother Frances Curteis (1816/7-1887) might have been the Mrs Curteis to whom CMY addressed a letter of 1878, but Elizabeth A. Curteis seems more plausible

title: James Batten Coulthard
text:
(b.1785/6)
maternal grandfather of Frances (Walter) Yonge
He was the father of father of Mary Emma, James Batten and Frances Charlotte Hilare Coulthard. Deirdre Le Faye, Jane Austen’s Letters, 511, calls him James Battin Coulthard (1782-1856). The Mrs Coulthard whom Jane Austen called (11 April 1805), 99, ‘a good-hearted, friendly woman’, was, Le Faye thinks, his stepmother Frances Barlow (1764-1829), who married his father Thomas Coulthard (1756-1811) in 1784. Perhaps there was some connection with Hilare Barlow (d.1757), second wife of the first Earl Nelson, who subsequently (1837) married Austen’s nephew George Thomas Knight . [1841 census]

title: Amy Augusta Jackson (Lawford) Coleridge
text:
(India 1853-1933)
second wife of the 1st Lord Coleridge
She had been married very young (1870) to her first cousin John Augustus Seymour Morse Davies (b.India 1842/3); the marriage, which had taken place at Gretna Green, was declared void in 1878, the parties not having lived in Scotland for 21 days preceding the wedding. [Lawford v Davies (1878) 4 PD 61, 47 LJP 38 P, D and Admlty] This fact may sufficiently account for the disapproving tone of CMY's references to her.

title: Sir John Duke Coleridge
text:
1st Lord Coleridge
(3 Dec 1820-14 June 1894)
Lord Chief Justice, contributor to MP (1878)
He married first (11 Aug 1846), Jane Fortescue Seymour (1824/5-1878); and, secondly (1885) Amy Augusta Jackson Lawford (1853-1933), who had previously passed for some years as the wife of her cousin John Davies, but whose Gretna Green marriage had been declared void in 1878. He was knighted in December 1868 on becoming solicitor general; he was created (1874) Lord Coleridge on being made chief justice of the common pleas; he became Lord Chief Justice in 1880.  [ODNB]

title: Sir John Duke Coleridge
text:
1st Lord Coleridge
(3 Dec 1820-14 June 1894)
Lord Chief Justice, contributor to MP (1878)
He married first (11 Aug 1846), Jane Fortescue Seymour (1824/5-1878); and, secondly (1885) Amy Augusta Jackson Lawford (1853-1933), who had previously passed for some years as the wife of her cousin John Davies, but whose Gretna Green marriage had been declared void in 1878. He was knighted in December 1868 on becoming solicitor general; he was created (1874) Lord Coleridge on being made chief justice of the common pleas; he became Lord Chief Justice in 1880.  [ODNB]

title: Christabel Rose Coleridge
text:
(1843-14 Nov 1921)
biographer of CMY
Daughter of the Rev. Derwent Coleridge (1800-1883), headmaster (1826-41) of Helston Grammar School, Principal (1841-64) of St Mark's College, Chelsea, and Rector (1864-80) of Hanwell, Middlesex, and his wife Mary Simpson Pridham;  she was granddaughter of S. T. Coleridge.  The longest-serving member of the Gosling Society, she was called Chelsea Delf to 1863 and Chelsea China thereafter;  she acted as secretary and many members seem to have been recruited by her;  she was first cousin to Cobweb and Hedgerose; Ladybird, Gurgoyle, Pena and Cricket were more distant relations.  The move to Hanwell took place in August 1864. In 1873 she was evidently helping her brother Ernest run a school in his house at Chiddingfold. Contributor to (including Debatable Ground) and later editor of MP. From 1890 she was editor of Friendly Leaves (Jan 1876 to June 1917) a magazine, originally edited by Anne Mercier, produced by the Girls' Friendly Society (founded 1875). Will proved £4615 [Hainton, Unknown Coleridge;  DNB; will]

title: Alice Mary Coleridge
text:
(1846-1907)
member of the Gosling Society, cousin of CMY
Much the youngest daughter of Francis Coleridge (1794-1854); her mother sank into depression after her birth and she was brought up by her elder sister Harriet Duke (Coleridge) Lowe (b.1829/30), wife of the Rev. Edward Clarke Lowe (1823-1912) one of the moving spirits in the Woodard Schools movement for the education of the middle classes.  She lived with them at The College, Hurstpierpoint, and was a Gosling as ‘Gurgoyle’ 1863-after 1869.  She became Lady Warden of St. Anne’s Abbot’s Bromley, a Woodard girls’ school, c. 1874 and spent the rest of her life there. Her brother Arthur Duke Coleridge was father of Mary E. Coleridge, the poet.  She was CMY’s goddaughter.  It looks from the Barnacle as if she spent some time living with Mildred and/or Mary Coleridge.  [ODNB; Rice, Story of St. Mary’s Abbot’s Bromley; Coleridge, Story of a Devonshire House]

title: Eliza Callahan Cleveland
text:
(1839-1914)
American correspondent of CMY
She is described as contributing sketches of good works among negroes to Mission Life 1867. She and her mother are mentioned as friends in Elizabeth Sewell's autobiography, which was probably how the correspondence with CMY arose.
She was the daughter of Sarah Paine Perkins and Henry Russell Cleveland (1808-1843) and was brought up in the New England political and literary establishment. She was the niece of the art collector Edward Newton Perkins (1820-1899) and was travelling with him and her mother in Italy in 1859 when they were attacked by local revolutionaries, causing a diplomatic incident.
Eugene Hollahan 'Intertextual Bondings Between \“The Wreck of the Hesperus\” and \“The Wreck of the Deutschland\”' Texas Studies in Literature and Language 33 (1991) discusses her correspondence with Longfellow which she communicated to CMY who published it in MP.
There is a large collection of the Cleveland and Perkins papers in the New York Public Library.

title: Harriet Catherine (Sweet) Carr
text:
(1804/5-1895)
contributor to MP (1861)
She married (1825) James Joshua Carr and was the mother of Harriet Catherine (Carr) Romanoff. A friend and neighbour of Ann Carter Smith, she lived at the Warren, Charlton. She contributed an Easter story to MP (1861). A monument in St. George’s Beckenham commemorates James Joshua CARR of Old Charlton Kent 22 May 1865 aged 67. Harriet Catherine Carr widow of above 27 August 1895 aged 90. Samuel Sidney Carr 13 August 1842 aged 1 year 9 months. Captain James Sweet Carr of H M Bombay Staff Corps 28 February 1864 aged 31 sons of James Joshua and Harriet Catherine Carr. Also Janet Susan Carr youngest daughter of J J and H C Carr 16 July 1919 aged 75. Also Harriet Catherine ROMANOFF daughter of J J and H C Carr 10 August 1897 aged 69 at Kishinieff Russia.

title: Hannah (Rogers) Carey
text:
(b. Dublin 1838/9)
widow of CMY’s cousin
The daughter of W. Rogers, she married (1860), James Arthur Yonge (1822-1864), barrister, third son of CMY’s Eton uncle. Hannah (Rogers) Yonge remarried Charles Egerton Carey in the Isle of Wight in 1874 and was living with him in Botley, Hants. in 1891, and as his widow with her sister in 1901. One letter to her refers to Stoke Lodge, which seems to have been afterwards occupied by James Arthur Yonge's younger brother George Edward Yonge (1824-1904).

title: Phoebe Anne Allen
text:
(1850-1933)
member of the Gosling Society and contributor to MP
Daughter of the Rev. John Collins Allen, Vicar of Hawley, Hants 1878-86, she was a Gosling as ‘Gridiron’. Living at Finchcox, Kilndown, Staplehurst (10 July 1870) and Hinton Martel Rectory, Wimborne (December 1876), she was perhaps a governess. She was author of 45 odd novels, stories for SPCK and translations. She was lame. [Vital Records Index; 1881 census, Will;  Alumni Oxonienses; Who's Who in Lit 1906-34; BL cat]

title: Geraldine Butt
text:
(b. Winchester 1854)
member of the Gosling Society, contributor to MP (1874)
She was author of fiction 1876-95, some of her publications are with her sister Beatrice May Butt (b. London 1853). She was probably a Gosling as ‘Staddacombe’ as CMY wrote (May 24th 1875) to Christabel Coleridge supposing so. She married (1878) John Campbell Robertson Glasgow (1844-1913) and was the author of further works as Geraldine Robertson-Glasgow. In the 1871 census she was living in Staffordshire with Beatrice and several other sisters and their mother Geraldine May Butt (Quebec 1831-1898). Her father was Lt.-Col. Thomas Bromhead Butt. She was probably the Geraldine Glasgow who contributed to MP (1897). [LG 1952]

title: Emily Lawrence
text:
1831/2-1914
SPG missionary at Tamatave, Madagascar.
She was the founder of several girls' schools in Madagascar, and was invalided home by the SPG in 1899. She had also worked in Mauritius. She died in an SPG hostel in Worthing. Death announcement, The Times (14 October 1914).

title: The Rev. Marsham Frederic Argles
text:
(1852-1883)
missionary, fellow of St. John's College, Oxford, third son of [[person:57]Julia Argles]
Eric James, The House of My Friends: Memories and Reflections (London: Continuum 2003), 133, states that Marsham Argles went out to join the Oxford mission in Calcutta as one of the founder members of the Brotherhood of the Epiphany. 'But, in 1883 Argles' health had broken down, and he died a fortnight after his return to England.' His sister Edith Argles was subsequently secretary of the Oxford Mission. [Alumni Oxonienses, census returns, birth and death registers; http://www.oxford-mission.org/archives/p4/art0.html] He is not to be confused with his elder brother, the Rev. Canon George Marsham Argles (1841-1920), Rector of St. Clement's York 1871-1919.

title: Miss Redgrave
text:
writing for MP in 1862
CMY's correspondent may possibly be the Elizabeth Redgrave whose The Word and the Work, or, The Harmony of Scripture with Geological Discoveries (London: Mozley 1860) was praised in MP (January 1860), 111. It was described as 'a series of conversations between a clergyman and a mechanic, intended to popularize Hugh Miller's views of the Periods of Creation, and to show how they may be reconciled with the Scriptural narrative.'

title: Anna Butler
text:
(b.1825/6)
sister of the Very Rev. William John Butler, contributor to MP (1852)
An obscure figure but an important early contributor to MP, she seems to have shared the family scholarly interests, being a student of German and Danish and author of a book CMY refers to as your ‘Scandinavian book’  in early 1856.  The letters in the Fairclough collection are clearly addressed to a sister of William Butler, who had two, Frances, who married in 1843 and died in 1851, and Anna (b.1825/6), who was still alive in 1892, author of Likes and Dislikes: Some Passages in the Life of Emily Marsden (1858) and several other works published as by ‘The author of Likes and Dislikes’, viz., Philip Bezant, or, Is Revenge Sweet? (1859), The Brotherhood of St. John the Evangelist at Berlin (1859), Chilcote Park, or, The Sisters (1860) and The Post Office Window, Being a Tale of the Night School (1862). The identification is confirmed by payments to Miss A. Butler in the bank account.

title: The Rev. Charles Bathurst Woodman
text:
(1807-1895)
Moravian Minister
Woodman seems to have been a well-off eccentric, who went mad towards the end of his life. He inherited some of the correspondence of Jonathan Swift, and published on that subject. At one time he was a minister in the Moravian church.

title: Maria Edith Walker
text:
(Middlesex 1843-1938)
correspondent of CMY
The letters from CMY to Miss M. E. Walker were presented to West Sussex Record Office in 1975 by the latter's niece Bertha Yeats, who was then 91. [Information from Alison McCann, West Sussex Record Office] Bertha Margaret F. Yeats (1883-1976) was the daughter of the Rev. George Yeats (b. Yorks. 1826/7), Vicar of Heworth, York, and Rosa Bertha Walker (1852-1900). Mrs Yeats's sister, Maria Edith Walker, was therefore the recipient of the letters from CMY. The Walker sisters were the daughters of Francis Walker (1809-1874), entomologist, and his wife and cousin Mary Elizabeth Ford (b. 1814/15), daughter of John Ford of Ellel Hall, Lancashire and Elizabeth Lawson. Francis Walker, son of John Walker (1766-1824) was a member of a family of rich and philanthropic brewers living at Arnos Grove, Southgate, Middlesex. The Miss Lawrence referred to in the correspondence, grandmother of Maria Walker, who had been at school with Frances Mary Yonge, was perhaps the wife of John Walker.

third son of CMY's cousin Dr. [[person:1622]John Francis Duke Yonge]
Stephen Yonge had emigrated to Melbourne, Australia, by 1864.
The Gentleman's Magazine (1866), 399 records the marriage (18 July 1866) 'At the Roman Catholic Cathdral, Plymouth, Stephen Duke Yonge, esq., of Plymstock, to Madeline Bartlet, fourth daughter of the late Lieut.-Col.George Smith, Bombay Native Infantry, of The Lodge, Plymton.'
However he does not appear in the 1871 or later English censuses, and in 1897 CMY evidently thought he was in California.
According to the Probate Records the will was proved in 1873 of 'Madeline Bartlett Yonge (Wife of Stephen Duke Yonge) formerly of Ladye Park near Liskeard in the County of Cornwall but late of Lower Lake, Lake County California . . who died 19 November 1872 at Lower Lake'.
According to data assembled by family genealogists at http://www.ilind.net/ancestry/jfdyDesc/p1.htm#i754, Stephen Yonge seems to have had two children.

title: Anne Badcock Buckle
text:
(1845-1871)
member of the Gosling Society
She was a Gosling to 1863, but her alias is unknown.  Daughter of Captain Edmund Buckle (1808-1846), Indian army officer and historian, and Anne Nicolls, she lived at 9 New Ormond St, Queens Square, London.  Her father’s aunt Anne married Col Stanhope Badcock and was great-grandmother of fellow-gosling Paulina Martyn. [LG 1952 Buckle, 1861 census]

title: Miss Bowles
text:
recommending a cook, 1890
This Miss Bowles was evidently a neighbour of Anne Sturges Bourne. The [[person:2]Elizabeth Savage] who was CMY's cook in 1891 had been born in Eling, which is near Anne Sturges Bourne's home at Testwood, but unfortunately in 1881 she was not working for anyone called Bowles, so it is impossible to determine either the identity of Miss Bourne or whether Savage was the cook in question.

title: Catherine Anna Mona (Macartney) Brougham
text:
(1804/5-1892)
contributor to MP (1888)
MS Girton College, Cambridge, To A.D. Innes (9 June 1888), identifies the contributor of an article signed 'C.A.W.B.' as 'Mrs Brougham/ Castlehaven Rectory/ Castletownsend'. The article is signed ‘C.A.W.B.’, and refers to ‘my mother, Lady M.’ and to the author as ‘aged’. The initials were evidently misprinted, and the author Catherine Anna Mona Brougham (1804/5-1892), widow of the Rev. Henry Brougham (d.1831), Rector of Tallow, and daughter of Sir John Macartney and his second wife Catherine Burgh. Two other articles appeared in the Monthly Packet in 1889 over the initials 'C.A.M.B.' Mrs Brougham was probably living with a clerical son, because E. Œ. Somerville, who lived in Castletownsend, refers (10 September 1888) to 'a hideous tennis function at the Broughams.' Selected Letters of Somerville and Ross ed. G. Lewis (London: Faber 1989), 118. Earlier 'Recollections of Parochial Work in Ireland' had appeared in MP 1864-5, and the bank account shows payments to Mrs Brougham of £11 (20 April 1863); £10 (12 March 1864); £5 15 s. (29 August 1864); £8 (31 Jan 1865); £3.3s. (23 February 1866). We are grateful to Mr Peter Wyly for supplying her dates of birth and death. [See also PB Brougham]

title: Mary Eliza Bramston
text:
(1841-2 February 1912)
contributor to MP (1870-96), novelist
Daughter of the Rev. John Bramston by his first marriage and half-sister of Anna Bramston, she was a prolific writer of fiction and non-fiction, generally for young women: she collaborated with CMY and others on Astray. She seems to have acted for some years as governess to the daughters of Archbishop Benson. Several of her works, such as Everingham Girls (1886)and Apples of Sodom (1889), portray intense same-sex relationships. She died at Kinshot Street Cross, Winchester, and left £8253. [Journal of Clarissa Trant, NUC, wills index]

title: Anna Rachel Bramston
text:
(Witham 1848/9-1931)
contributor to MP (1887)
She was the daughter of the Rev. John Bramston by his second wife, and half-sister of Mary Eliza Bramston. In the 1891 census she was living at Witham Close, Kingsgate St., Winchester with her widowed mother Anna Bramston aged 84. She seems to have lived later with the novelist Amelie Claire Leroy, and they adopted a daughter called Juliette (perhaps a relation of Leroy’s?). The two of them published two books on the history of Winchester. They were closely involved in the foundation of the Winchester High School (now St. Swithin’s School). She was on the Diocesan Council of the GFS in 1878, she and CMY in charge of the literature department.

title: Sarah Paine (Perkins) Cleveland
text:
(d.1893)
mother of Eliza Callahan Cleveland
Mother and daughter were friends of Elizabeth Sewell's; see her autobiography (1907), 159, 208. See also A Selection from the Writings of Henry R. Cleveland, with a Memoir (Boston: private distribution 1844). She married Henry Russell Cleveland (1808-1843) in 1838 and they had one daughter (b. 1839). She was the sister of the art collector Edward Newton Perkins (1820-1899) and she and her daughter were with him in Perugia in 1859 when they were attacked by local revolutionaries, causing a diplomatic incident.
A large collection of their papers is in the New York Public Library.

title: Lieutenant George Dobson Willoughby
text:
(1828/9-11 May 1857)
The text of his memorial in Bath Abbey is at http://glosters.tripod.com/IM8.htm
'Sacred to the memory of George Dobson Willoughby, 1st Lieut. Bengal Artillery and Commissary of Ordnance at Delhi, aged 28 years. As a brave and zealous soldier he stood firm in defence of his post intrusted to him, and when resistance failed blew up the Delhi Magazine on 11 May 1857 to prevent its falling into the hands of the mutineers and rebels. Burnt and wounded he subsequently fell a prey to insurgents. This tablet is erected by his sorrowing relatives.'
CMY included his story in A Book of Golden Deeds, having read about him in The Times.

title: ‘Bath Brick’
text:
'Bath Brick' made her first appearance in MP in May 1876 as a participant in the [[other:116]Spider] competitions, when she was commended for a life of the Cid. In June 1876 she appears as ‘A Bath Brick’. Requested to describe ‘A Parish Church’ she chose Clevedon (September 1876): 'on one of the rounded headlands of the Bristol Channel, between Brean Down and Portishead, nestles the old parish church of C____ , dedicated to St. Andrew.'
In March 1879 she described Wrington in answer to 'Describe the most interesting place in your neighbourhood'. Therefore it seems reasonable to assume that she resided in north Somerset. As well as being a regular contestant in the Spider competitions, Bath Brick was also a frequent correspondent of the magazine. She is mentioned in the poem written by Mary Anderson Morshead to commemorate the ending of the Spiders in March 1887. Subsequently she took part in the Debateable Ground essay society. Her last appearance was in September 1896.
As to her identity, one possibility is that she was Emma Caroline Scarth (Bath 1850-?1921), younger daughter of the Rev. Harry Scarth, Rector of Wrington.

title: Olive Collins
text:
(b.1841/2)
daughter of George and Martha Collins
The Collins family consisted of George Collins, (b. 1818/9) of the Post Office, Otterbourne, groom and gardener, his wife Martha (Andrews), postmistress, and their children George (b. 1839/40), Charles (b.1840/1) Olive (b. 1841/2), Elizabeth (Bessie) and Anne (b. 1846/7).
At the time of the 1861 census Olive was housemaid to CMY's friend the Rev. W. J. Butler, Vicar of Wantage. She married Francis Pond in 1869. In 1891 she was still living in Wantage, working as a cook in a boys' boarding school.

title: George Richmond
text:
(1809-1896)
artist
Two portraits by Richmond of CMY exist, one in the NPG, the other a sketch of her head. He also painted WCY (the picture reproduced in Coleridge Life), Keble (at the behest of Sir William Heathcote) and Mary Ann Moberly. In Richmond's archive in the Royal Academy are some letters about the Keble commission, including one from Heathcote telling Richmond to ignore WCY's attempts to interfere with the design.

title: Cordelia (Garstin) Bargus [formerly Colborne]
text:
(1751-1791)
first wife of CMY's grandfather
No relation to CMY, this lady is all the same the key to the complex cousinhood which surrounded her. She had two children, John and Cordelia Anne, by her first marriage to Samuel Colborne. She then married the Rev. Thomas Bargus, and died giving birth to their daughter Alethea Henrietta Bargus. He remarried, and had a daughter, CMY's mother Frances Mary Bargus. Cordelia Colborne married the Rev. Duke Yonge. Alethea married the latter's cousin the Rev John Yonge. John Colborne married Elizabeth Yonge (sister of the Rev. John Yonge). Lastly, Frances married William Crawley Yonge (brother of the Rev. Duke Yonge).

title: The Hon. Mildred Mary (Coleridge) Adams
text:
(1847-14 January 1929)
member of the Gosling Society as ‘Ladybird’
The only daughter of John Duke Coleridge, later 1st Lord Coleridge, and Jane Fortescue Seymour (d. 1878), she married (24 June 1885) Charles Warren Adams. She had a row with her father about her marriage which was made public because Adams sued her brother Bernard for libel.  She was a Gosling as Ladybird 1859- c. 1866;   [PB, Coleridge, Story of a Devonshire House; The Times reports of the libel trial 21, 22 November 1884, 5, 25 June 1885.]

title: Mary Isabella (Bogue) Smith
text:
(before 1823-1878)
daughter of Captain Richard Bogue
It seems reasonable to identify Mary Bogue with this person, who married on 22 November 1838 the Reverend Francis Smith (1810-1851), Rector of Tarrant Rushton, 5th son of Sir John Wyldbore Smith, 2nd Bt. and Elizabeth Anne Marriott. Richard Bogue was JBY’s godfather, see Coleridge, Life 74. He was probably FMY's first cousin, son (or perhaps grandson) of her father's aunt Mary (Bargus) Bogue.

Letter 3396

title: Charlotte Esther Sophia Guille
text:
1852-1939
member of the Gosling Society as Bessie (or Befsie)
This letter was found in the Guille autograph album, and was almost certainly addressed to one of the eight daughters of the Rev. George de Cartaret Guille, Rector of Torrington, and his wife Sophia Stevens, heiress of Cross, near Torrington. The most likely candidate seems to be the third sister, Charlotte Esther Sophia Guille Other possibilities among the sisters are the eldest, Louisa Margaret A. Guille (b. 1849) who m. (1877) the Rev. Frederick Colby and then (1905) William Secker; or the second, Marian Elizabeth Guille (1851-1931) who married (1881) William George Le Cocq, RN (1842/3-1913). Their younger sister Rosa Caroline (b.1858) became (1878) the wife of the poet William Johnson Cory.

title: Richard Bargus
text:
c.1722-1805
great-grandfather of CMY
CMY describes him as 'a naval officer, who after sailing round the world with Lord Anson, had settled at Fareham, in Hampshire' (Coleridge, Life 4). He died in his 83rd year at Fareham (Gentleman’s Magazine, 1805, 389). He married, first (Rowner 1745), Frances Eyer and, secondly (Fareham 1759), Ann Lock. His second wife’s death is also recorded in the Gentleman’s Magazine (1814) 87. According to the Hampshire Allegations for marriage licences they were 36 and 25 at marriage. It appears from the IGI that the Rev. Thomas Bargus (christened Fareham 1753) and Mary (Bargus) Bogue were the children of his first marriage, Sophia (Bargus) Leeke, Charlotte Bargus and John Lock Bargus (b. 1762/3) of the second. There may have been other children.

title: Sophia (Bargus) Leeke
text:
1761/2-1847
great-aunt of CMY
The European Magazine 14 (1788) 231 recorded the marriage of Samuel Leake, esq., of the Isle of Wight, to Sophia Bargus, youngest daughter of Richard Bargus esq. of Fareham. She appears to have been a half-sister of the Rev. Thomas Bargus. The Gentleman’s Magazine 182 (1847) 563 records her death at Cheltenham aged 85, spelling it Leeke, and stating that she was the mother of Captain Sir Henry Leeke, R.N. (1794-1870, ODNB,/i>). Lady Tucker, to whom CMY refers, would thus be her daughter and FMY’s first cousin.

title: The Rev. Charles Burell Yonge
text:
(1866-1943)
Rector of Newton Ferrars, cousin of CMY
He was the son of the Rev. Duke Yonge of Puslinch and of Charlotte Cordelia Pode, who were both first cousins of CMY, on her mother's and father's sides respectively, and were each other's second cousins. Charles Yonge married (1899) Catherine Ada Helena Bowen. He is remembered for the collection of rare crocuses he naturalized in the churchyard at Newton Ferrars.

title: Julian Bargus Yonge
text:
(31 Jan 1830-17 October 1891)
brother of CMY
He was educated at Eton and Balliol College Oxford (BA 1853). He joined the Rifle Brigade 1851, entered the Middle Temple 1855. He married (9 Sept 1858) Emma Frances Walter (1839-1913). He was a J.P. for Hampshire; sold Otterbourne House to Major Robert Scarlett in 1891 and died ‘a few days later’ (John Keble’s Parishes, 149), at 70 Kensington Gardens Square in the presence of his son Maurice [death certificate] but according to the probate of his will at 2 Radnor Place Hyde Park.

Letter 3370

title: Dr. John Francis Duke Yonge M.D.
text:
(1814-25 Dec 1879)
cousin of CMY
The son of the Rev. Duke Yonge, Vicar of Antony, he married first (Brussels 12 August 1839) a widow with two children, Elizabeth Alice (Holmes) Reed (1799/1800-1863) and, secondly (1864) Mary Jones.  He seems to have converted to Roman Catholicism.  For that reason he was not welcome at WCY’s funeral.
By his first marriage he had three sons, Francis Arthur Holmes Yonge, James Frederick Moore Yonge (b. Coblentz 1841/2?) and Stephen Duke Yonge (Coblentz 1844/5-1914). In the 1871 census he and his second wife are living in Cornwall with three small grandsons, born in Mauritius, and probably the children of Francis, who had gone to live there. The two younger sons had moved to Melbourne, Australia, by 1864.
One of the 'grandsons', Eugene Yonge (b. Mauritius 1867/8) appears in the 1881 census in Plymstock with his uncle the Rev. Theophilus Jones (1822/3-1886), Vicar of Bradstone, and as a medical student in the 1891 census. [census; Alumni Oxonienses An obituary of 'Eugene Stephen Yonge, M.D.Edin., Physician, Manchester Hospital For Consumption' appeared in the The British Medical Journal 1 (Feb. 3, 1912), p. 280; advertisement Morning Post 16 April 1864, 1]

title: Dr. John Francis Duke Yonge M.D.
text:
(1814-25 Dec 1879)
cousin of CMY
The son of the Rev. Duke Yonge, Vicar of Antony, he married first (Brussels 12 August 1839) a widow with two children, Elizabeth Alice (Holmes) Reed (1799/1800-1863) and, secondly (1864) Mary Jones.  He seems to have converted to Roman Catholicism.  For that reason he was not welcome at WCY’s funeral.
By his first marriage he had three sons, Francis Arthur Holmes Yonge, James Frederick Moore Yonge (b. Coblentz 1841/2?) and Stephen Duke Yonge (Coblentz 1844/5-1914). In the 1871 census he and his second wife are living in Cornwall with three small grandsons, born in Mauritius, and probably the children of Francis, who had gone to live there. The two younger sons had moved to Melbourne, Australia, by 1864.
One of the 'grandsons', Eugene Yonge (b. Mauritius 1867/8) appears in the 1881 census in Plymstock with his uncle the Rev. Theophilus Jones (1822/3-1886), Vicar of Bradstone, and as a medical student in the 1891 census. [census; Alumni Oxonienses An obituary of 'Eugene Stephen Yonge, M.D.Edin., Physician, Manchester Hospital For Consumption' appeared in the The British Medical Journal 1 (Feb. 3, 1912), p. 280; advertisement Morning Post 16 April 1864, 1]

title: Elizabeth Wordsworth
text:
(23 June 1840-30 Nov 1932)
contributor to MP (1871-1893), principal of Lady Margaret Hall
Daughter of the Rt. Rev. Christopher Wordsworth, she was appointed Lady Principal of Lady Margaret Hall in 1878. She contributed a poem to MP in 1871 under the name 'VERITAS' and probably was the author of another 16 poems which appeared under that pseudonym to February 1883.
[Battiscombe, Reluctant Pioneer]

title: The Rt. Rev. Charles Wordsworth
text:
(1806-1892)
Bishop of St Andrews, Dunkeld and Dunblane
He became bishop in 1852. Before that he had travelled in France with Roundell Palmer in 1834, and married (1835) Charlotte Day, who died giving birth to a daughter Charlotte Emmeline (b. 1839). He was second master of Winchester College 1835-1846, and author of Christian Boyhood at a Public School (1846). He was first headmaster of Trinity College Glenalmond, 1847. He married, secondly (28 October 1846), Katharine Mary Barter (d. 23 Apr 1897), eldest daughter of the Rev. William Barter, Rector of Burghclere, and niece of Warden Barter; he had 12 children by his second marriage. Like the Rt. Rev. Christopher Wordsworth, Bishop of Lincoln, he was a son of the poet’s brother Christopher, master of Trinity College, Cambridge. [ODNB]

text:

wife of the Rev. Robert Francis Wilson
She married (6 May 1847) the Rev. Robert Francis Wilson, Vicar of Rownhams (d. 1888).  The Court and Lady’s Magazine (April 1847) 57 has ‘Trench, Maria, d. of the rev. Frederick S. Trench and lady Helen Trench, of Kilmorony, to the rev. Francis Wilson, son of Thomas Wilson esq. formerly M. P. for the city of London; at the parish church of Athy, by the rev. John Keble, vicar of Hursley Hants, May 6 [1847]’ She was 1st cousin of T.R.F. Cooke Trench and, through her mother, of Sir William Heathcote's children by his first marriage. Frances Dorothy (Grimston) Moor was her first cousin, daughter of her aunt Jane Trench. [PB 1914 Ashtown; Erminois]

title: Florence Wilford
text:
(Woolwich 29 Feb 1836-?1897)
novelist, contributor to MP (1866-83), member of the Gosling Society
Daughter of Major-General Edmund Neal Wilford (1800-1881), RA, Governor of the Royal Military Academy, Woolwich, until his retirement, she was a Gosling as ‘Turkscap’ from June 1865; her address then was c/o Mrs Chrystall's,. St. Cross, Winchester. In the 1851 census, she was 15 and was staying at Bromley Kent, in the company of Maria Cristall, whom CMY describes as her aunt.  In 1881 she and her elder sister Emma, 48, were living with their father in St. Leonards; in 1891 she and Emma were staying in Bournemouth.  Her Royal Literary Fund file indicates that she had suffered from mental illness intermittently since 1883 and was in a private asylum in 1889 when CMY wrote a letter to them on her behalf. (Her application was refused on the grounds ‘authorship insufficient’, which seems rather harsh since she had published sixteen books since 1858.) CMY assured Bullock (16 March 1895) that she was well and at home with her sister. She was probably the Florence Wilford who died at Keynsham, Glos. in 1897. [IGI; RLF application 2311, born at Woolwich]

title: Margaret Wilcox
text:
(1836-1887)
cousin of Lewis Carroll
Margaret Wilcox was the daughter of William Wilcox (1813-1872), Collector of Customs, and his wife Mary Anne Marwood (1813-1870), half-sister of the Rev. Charles Dodgson (1800-1868), father of Lewis Carroll.
They lived at Whitburn, Northumberland, near CMY's friends the Harrisons. There were 14 children, of which she was the third.

title: Miss White
text:
calling on FMY (24 Sept 1846)
She may be the Miss White, later Mrs Gustard, dead by 1861, listed as a contributor to the Hursley Magazine in 1848. The latter was perhaps Margaretta Sarah White, daughter of the Rev. John White (1761/2-1841), Vicar of Exminster, who married (Mangalore 4 December 1850) Captain H. F. Gustard, 6th Regiment of Native Infantry (Morning Chronicle, 22 January 1851; Alumni Oxonienses). Or she may have been a member of the White family who occupied Ampfield House until at least the 1881 census, whose head in 1881 was another, different Rev. John White (b.Ampfield 1817). [Moor, Erminois, on Ampfield House]

title: Susanna Warren
text:
(b. Edmonton 1814/5)
author of fiction and non-fiction as ‘S.W.’
In the 1851 census she was living with her elder sister Selina Warren (b. 1812/3) at Hazlewood Farm, Hennock, near Bovey Tracey, Devon; she was staying with nephew William Henderson, woollen manufacturer, at 3 South Bailey Durham at the time of the 1861 census;  in the 1871 census she was again at Hazlewood, Hennock, with her eldest sister Charlotte Warren (b.1787/8). It seems possible that in the 1860s she worked for the Rev. John Hawksley in Clifton as a governess. She was author of fiction and non-fiction (tracts?) from 1860, under the initials S.W., for the SPCK.

title: Lieutenant Colonel Edward Walter
text:
(1804- 10 Dec 1862)
father-in-law of JBY
He retired in 1851 as Lt-Col 3rd Bombay Light Cavalry. He married, first, Mary Emma Coulthard and, secondly (1853), Caroline Jeannette [or Janetta] Bignell, who married subsequently the Rev. James Beck. He was the son of W. Walter, and descended from John Walter the founder of The Times. His daughter Frances Walter married JBY; his daughter Gertrude lived with CMY from the 1870s; CMY also often mentions his daughters Mary (Walter) Woolcombe and Louisa (Walter) Halliday.  [BL OIOC MSS and East India Register Jan 1863; marriage index]

title: Laura Belinda Charlotte (Jewry) Valentine
text:
(1816?-1899)
contributor to MP, publishers’ editor
She was born at Portsea, Hants., and gave different ages in several census returns. A governess in 1841; she married (1853) the Rev. Richard John Scobell Valentine (d.1853/4), curate in charge of St. Mary, Portsea. The birth to her of a posthumous stillborn son was reported in the Morning Chronicle (1 July 1854). By 1861 she was living with her mother (widow of a lieutenant RN) at the Naval Asylum, Battersea, with her unmarried sister Mary Jewry. She worked for Warne as a children’s books editor.

title: The Rt. Rev. Charles Richard Sumner
text:
(1790-1874)
Bishop of Winchester.
He was unpopular in Hursley as he refused to ordain the Rev. Peter Young. He seems to have been a conscientious but mildly Evangelical bishop who shared the alarm felt by many at the extremism of the Tractarians. He retired owing to ill health in 1869. He was father of the Rt. Rev. [[person:1382]George Henry Sumner].

title: Jane Mary (Dampier) Smith-Dampier
text:
(1836/7-Dec 1906)
novelist, neighbour of CMY
Jane Mary Dampier and her sister Emily Dampier (b.1838/9) lived at Twyford House, Twyford. This accounts for her visiting (1870) with Mrs Buston, the wife of the vicar of Twyford. Jane married (1873) Alfred Fowler Smith and adopted the surname Smith-Dampier: she was the author of a novel, Journal of Lady Beatrix Graham (1870) which was published with an introduction by CMY. Some of her work was published as by Mrs Fowler Smith. In the 1871 census Jane Mary Dampier was living unmarried at Twyford House with her sister Emily Dampier (b.1838/9), describes her occupation as ‘keeping things in order’. By the time of her death she was calling herself Smith-Dampier. They were daughters of John L. Dampier and Margaret Dampier; he was Vice-Warden of the Stannaries, and died 1851-61. [COPAC; 1871 census; 1841 census; death register]

title: Georgina Alethe (Polson) Adams
text:
(1838-?1880)
first cousin once removed to CMY, first wife of Charles Warren Adams
The Times (11 January 1862) reported the marriage ‘at Cape-town, cape of good Hope, Charles Warren Adams, Esq, son of the late Mr. Serjeant Adams to Georgiana Alethe, youngest daughter of the Rev. Hugh Polson.’ The death of a Georgiana Alethe Adams aged 2 was registered in Fulham in 1880. According to the Bristol Mercury and Daily Post (24 November 1884) Charles Warren Adams stated in court that he had lived happily with his wife who had died of cancer. He had always supported his daughter. The refusal of the Bishop of Cape Town to marry them was a mistake, and he had afterwards been reconciled to her family.

text:
(1838-?1880)
first cousin once removed to CMY, first wife of Charles Warren Adams
The Times (11 January 1862) reported the marriage ‘at Cape-town, cape of good Hope, Charles Warren Adams, Esq, son of the late Mr. Serjeant Adams to Georgiana Alethe, youngest daughter of the Rev. Hugh Polson.’ The death of a Georgiana Alethe Adams aged 2 was registered in Fulham in 1880. According to the Bristol Mercury and Daily Post (24 November 1884) Charles Warren Adams stated in court that he had lived happily with his wife who had died of cancer. He had always supported his daughter. The refusal of the Bishop of Cape Town to marry them was a mistake, and he had afterwards been reconciled to her family.

title: The Rt. Rev. John Richardson Selwyn
text:
(1844-12 Feb 1898)
son of the Rt. Rev. George Augustus Selwyn
He was educated at St John’s College Auckland, Eton, Trinity College Cambridge (BA 1866). He was ordained in 1869 and married, first (1872), Clara Innes. He succeeded Patteson as Bishop of Melanesia (1877). He married, secondly (1885), Annie Catherine Mort, retired and became Master of Selwyn Coll Cambridge [DNZB, ODNB] His biography makes it clear he was in England June 1889-January 1890, and then returned for good to Europe in September 1891; this is obscured in ODNB.

title: Margaret Eliza Roberts
text:
(1833-1919 Montreux)
author of Mademoiselle Mori
She was educated by her stepfather, the Rev. Henry Latham (1794-1866 ODNB);  she was a contributor to MP (1864-92). Her most successful work was Mademoiselle Mori:  A Tale of Modern Rome (1860), which CMY encouraged Parker to print; she published as ‘the author of Mademoiselle Mori’. CMY had a plan to co-author German history with her; she suffered a strain of the hip in 1873; and lived in Torquay. She has titles in the British Library Catalogue 1857-1901. A letter in the Bodleian from Roberts to Bullock calls Grace Latham her cousin, and perhaps there was a blood relationship with some of the Lathams. (ODNB; Blain, Feminist Companion; Mary Harris, Frances Mary Peard says she died at Territet]

title: Mrs (Mason) Reeves
text:
(fl.22 July 1845)
schoolmistress
Probably Charlotte Mason, CMY’s former nurse, sister of the transported agitator Joseph Mason (1799-1863). She could be the Charlotte Reeves (1803/4-1871) who kept a lodging-house at Ventnor, IOW, but that person seems already to have been there in 1841. She could also be the ‘old nurse of mine who married a drum major’ mentioned in a letter to Ann Carter Smith (31 March 1864). Coleridge, Life, 80-1; John Keble’s Parishes, 96-7; Charlotte M. Yonge, An Old Woman’s Outlook in a Hampshire Village (London: Macmillan: 1892), 65-7; Joseph Mason: Assigned Convict 1831-1837ed. David Kent and Norma Townsend (Melbourne: Melbourne University Press 1996), 3.

She was living at Uffington, Faringdon, on income from Funds without a servant, with her mother Catherine Poole (b. 1807/8), in whose memory a chalice was given to Uffington Church; she became an associate of the Wantage sisterhood in 1869; she later became a governess and at the time of the 1881 census was living in Camberwell with her sister Harriet Poole (b. Mansfield, Nottingham 1843/4), also a governess. [Victoria County History; information from Wantage; census]

title: Jessie Peters
text:
member of the Gosling Society
She was a Gosling as ‘Firefly’ and lived at 4 Belmont, Brighton (a cul de sac off Dyke Road); she was probably daughter of Edward Peters who ran a 'Boarding School for young gentlemen' at that address. In the 1861 census the Gosling Paulina Martyn was living in Dawlish with her grandmother Sophia (Badcock) Coleridge and the latter's niece Sophia Peters, so possibly Jessie Peters was also a relation of some sort.

title: Charlotte Champion (Willyams) Pascoe
text:
(1782-20 May 1874)
writer
She was the daughter of James Willyams (1741-1828) and married the Rev. T. Pascoe, Vicar of St. Hilary. She was author with her sister Jane Louisa Willyams (d. unm) of a novel, Coquetry 3 vols (1818), and of Walks about St. Hilary which was published posthumously in 1879 edited by C.G.B. Rogers (her first cousin) and Mary Rogers, perhaps niece of the latter’s husband.  Administration of her will was granted to her nephew Humphry Grylls. Walter Scott had helped with the publication of Coquetry.  [will; LG 1906 Willyams, Rogers of Penrose;  Bibliotheca Cornubienses, has b.1781?]

title: John William Parker sr.
text:
(1792-1870)
publisher
He was printer to Cambridge University Press and the publisher of CMY’s early bestsellers of the 1850s. After the death of his son John William Parker, jr., in 1860 he continued in partnership with William Butler Bourn but then sold the firm to Longmans in 1863. The firm should not be confused with that of John Henry Parker of Oxford, who published Kenneth. [DLB 106]

title: Horatio (Bolton) Nelson
text:
3rd Earl Nelson
(1823-1913)
of Brickworth, Wiltshire
He was grandson of the admiral’s sister Mrs Bolton, brother of Lady Susan (Nelson) Blunt. His father had changed his surname from Bolton to Nelson in 1835 on succeeding to the title, which had been secured by special remainder to descendants of the admiral’s sisters. The 3rd Lord Nelson’s mother, Frances Eyre (d.1878), was a friend of FMY’s, and CMY therefore knew him and his sister as children.

(b. 1825/6)
social worker
Third of the five daughters of the Rev. Edward Barlee, Rector of Worlingworth, Suffolk, she was Lady superintendent of the Institution for the Employment of Needlewomen, Hinde St, founded 1860, as well as a contributor to the Athenaeum, the Englishwoman’s Journal and MP (1866), referred to 3 April 1863. [1851, 1861 census]

title: Ellen J. Millington
text:
(b.1818)
teacher and contributor to MP(1853-64)
Ellen J. Millington translated The Aesthetic and Miscellaneous Works of Frederick von Schlegel from the German in 1849 (reprinted a number of times up to 1891), Christian iconography, or, the history of Christian art in the Middle Ages by A.N. Didron from the French in 1851, and herself wrote Heraldry in History, Poetry, and Romance (1858) and Characteristics of the Gods of Greece. A Manual for Schoolgirls (1867). As well as the series of articles on heraldry, she contributed seven articles on ‘King Arthur and his Knights’ to the MP between 1861 and 1864. Daughter of Henry Millington (b. 1790/1), attorney's clerk [1851 census, in Bristol], she is described by CMY as an invalid ex-governess; in the 1861 census she was living with her mother (father absent but evidently alive) Emma Millington (b.1794/5), her sister Anna Gerard (b. 1818/9), widowed by 1851 census, keeping a tiny school of three pupils in Westbury on Trym. In the 1871 census her mother is Anna Millington, now a widow, Anna Gerard also now a widow, and the school bigger and occupying two houses in Clifton. She was living at Hawarden, Wales, with Anna Gerard, in the 1881 census. Her father is found in 1861 (not 1871) living with his son, the Rev. Thomas Street Millington (1821-1906), curate, later vicar of Woodhouse, Leics., and a prolific author of theology and boys’ fiction.

title: Christabel Rose Coleridge - ‘The eagle of one house is the goose of another’
text:
Date: 1864
Imprint: MP (December 1864), 602-626
Notes: The story appeared in MP under the initials 'C.D.'. It appeared in Coleridge's The Face of Carlyon and Other Stories, and is referred to in a a letter thanking her and enclosing a cheque.
A letter of uncertain date addressed to Elizabeth Charlotte Curteis, which refers to 'your eagle and goose story' cannot therefore refer to this story, and must relate to the Christmas number of 1872, which took the proverb as a theme.

title: Saturday Magazine, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Saturday Magazine, The
text:
Description
This periodical was founded in 1832 as a penny magazine for the working classes, under the auspices of the SPCK; its publisher was John W. Parker.

title: Penny Post, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Penny Post, The
text:
Description
Mentioned by CMY, perhaps rather surprisingly, as reaching the same readers as the MP.

title: Net Cast in Many Waters: Sketches from the Lives of Missionaries, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Net Cast in Many Waters: Sketches from the Lives of Missionaries, The
text:
Description
This periodical, which was founded in 1866 and continued after 1897 as The Net: The Monthly Magazine of the Zululand Missionary Association, was originally edited by CMY’s great friend Anne Mackenzie.

title: Morning Herald, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Morning Herald, The
text:
Description
This was a daily newspaper published in London which ran 1780-1869.

title: British Quarterly Review, The
text: Type: periodical

title: British Quarterly Review, The
text:
Description
Macmillan sent CMY a good review of The Dove in the Eagle’s Nest (4 July 1866).

title: Barnacle, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Barnacle, The
text:
Description
This was a manuscript magazine circulating among members of the Gosling Society. The surviving issues (incomplete series, 1863-1867)  are owned by Lady Margaret Hall, Oxford. They are described by Julia Courtney, ‘The Barnacle: A manuscript magazine of the 1860s’, in The Girl’s Own: Cultural Histories of the Anglo –American Girl 1830-1915 ed. Claudia Nelson and Lynne Vallone.  Arkansas and London, University of Georgia Press: 1994.

title: St. Luke’s Magazine
text: Type: periodical

title: St. Luke’s Magazine
text:
Description
Referred to (25 April 1872) as publishing articles by the Rt. Rev. C. J. Abraham about Bishop Patteson. The Exeter Diocesan Training College was known as St. Luke’s between 1854 and 1978. It seems probable that this periodical was associated with it, although no library with holdings from this period has been found.

title: Otterbourne Parish Magazine
text: Type: periodical

title: Otterbourne Parish Magazine
text:
Description
Hampshire Record Office holds a run of this periodical 1874-1932. References to CMY and her family are very frequent; her contributions not infrequent. Further investigation might be rewarding.

title: Newbery Magazine
text: Type: periodical

title: Newbery Magazine
text:
Description
This was a children’s magazine,

title: Monthly Paper of Sunday Teaching, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Monthly Paper of Sunday Teaching, The
text:
Description
This was edited by CMY throughout its existence, 1860-1875, and was intended to provide suitable material for Sunday School teachers, as she described it (21 May 1860) to Anne Sturges Bourne: ‘I am intending to set up a Monthly sheet of questions for Sunday Schools on the Gospels for each Sunday in the month, also questions on the Catechism, and a small sort of reading to the girl pupils, on some subject connected with the day - as the history of the preservation & MSS of the Bible on the 2nd Sunday in Advent - the full Jewish passover on the 1st after Easter - the Eastern shepherding ways on the Shepherd Sunday. Could you help me to any of these, adapted to the older classes of Sunday Schools just what you would say to your orphans. Not quite a sermon but more entertaining to young people. Septuagesima is to have the Isthmean games - and the 21st after Trinity the account of armour. Any other Sunday in the year, or any subject is open. I should be very glad of help, specially yours. Real anecdotes would do well, drawn into morals according with the Sunday, harvest or Christmas advice &c, but I forswear stories or I shall be eaten up and dissipated with them. It is to be on the model of the National Society’s Sunday School paper, only taking in churchisms which they are afraid of. The papers should not exceed a sheet of full sized note paper.’ Arthur Rawson Ashwell, Frances Mary Peard and Henrietta Fursdon are known contributors.

title: Norwich Union Insurance Company
text: Type: organization

title: Norwich Union Insurance Company
text:
Description
Founded in 1797, the firm is now part of Aviva, see www.aviva.co.uk

title: Macmillan’s Magazine
text: Type: periodical

title: Macmillan’s Magazine
text:
Description
This magazine ran 1859-1907. Two of CMY’s historical novels were serialized in it: The Dove in the Eagle’s Nest (May-December 1865) and The Chaplet of Pearls (November 1867-December 1868), and her series of articles ‘Children’s Literature of the Last Century’ (May-October 1869), 229-37, 302-10, 448-56.

title: Literary Churchman, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Literary Churchman, The
text:
Description
This periodical ran 1855-1892. CMY contributed to it a review of G. O. Trevelyan’s Cawnpore as well as one to Macmillan’s Magazine (letter 29 May 1865). She contributed an obituary of Bishop Patteson in 1871: ‘one that I could do from my heart of those vague In Memoriam things’ (mentioned to Fanny Patteson, 8 December 1871), and wrote other unidentified contributions, some sufficiently substantial for her to propose their inclusion in a projected volume of essays (To Alexander Macmillan, 20 March 1869). Its editor 1864-1876 was the Rev. Arthur Rawson Ashwell (1824–1879), of whom CMY thought very highly. He contributed both to MP and to the Monthly Packet of Sunday Teaching.

title: Great Grimsby Parish Magazine
text: Type: periodical

title: Great Grimsby Parish Magazine
text:
Description
A letter (MS Bodleian Library 1 October 1883) to the Rev. Charles Moor, who was curate of Great Grimsby 1882, suggests that she contributed a story from Langley Adventures to a magazine of some kind he was editing. North East Lincolnshire Archives hold (their reference 1007/65-67) parish magazines for All Saints, Great Grimsby, 1887-1981, which might be the right one.

title: Girls’ Friendly Society Associates’ Journal, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Girls’ Friendly Society Associates’ Journal, The
text:
Description
This periodical (1883-1919) was aimed at the upper-class associates, not at the Members of the GFS, for whom the sister journals Friendly Leaves and Friendly Work existed. Between 1881-3 it was entitled the Girls' Friendly Society Advertiser. It contains references to CMY's presence at meetings and comments by her, some letters from her (included in this edition) and articles signed by her.
An obituary of CMY, describing her GFS work, signed C. C. and probably the work of Christabel Coleridge, appeared in the issue for May 1901, p.75.

title: Family Friend, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Family Friend, The
text:
Description
This magazine (1849-1921) was mentioned by CMY, letter no 56, as widely read in cottages. See Charlotte Fell-Smith, ‘Philp, Robert Kemp Smith, ‘Philp, Robert Kemp (1819–1882)’, rev. Stephen Roberts, Oxford Dictionary of National Biography, Oxford University Press, 2004.

title: Events of the Month
text: Type: periodical

title: Events of the Month
text:
Description
This periodical was advertised as Events of the Month: A Magazine of News, Literature, Science, and General Information; Designed for Youthful Readers to be published by John and Charles Mozley, starting January 1st, 1864. Price sixpence.” It was edited by Henrietta Murray and ran from January 1864 to January 1865. CMY later described it to Macmillan (3 June 1864) as “a sort of school room Athenaeum”, and tried unsuccessfully to get him to take it over when the Mozleys abandoned it.(see Macmillan to CMY 17 January 1865). ‘Lost [sic] Heartsease Leaves’ was published in it in January 1865.

title: Cottagers’ Monthly Visitor
text: Type: periodical

title: Cottagers’ Monthly Visitor
text:
Description
This was a periodical which ran 1821-1856, designed for working-class readers, in which the Rt. Rev. George Davys was much interested and to which he contributed. CMY mentions (5 December 1838) that her family had ‘hatched up amongst us’ some articles for it, which must have been among her earliest appearances in print.

title: Constitutional Press
text: Type: periodical

title: Constitutional Press
text:
Description
This magazine, which ran 1859-60, was published by Saunders & Otley; they serialized Hopes and Fears.

title: Churchman’s Family Magazine, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Churchman’s Family Magazine, The
text:
Description
This periodical seems to have run 1863-1873. The publisher was Hogg. The Clever Woman of the Family was serialized in it (January 1864-April 1865).

title: Church Times, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Church Times, The
text:
Description
This is a High Church Anglican weekly newspaper founded in 1863. It still exists and its website is www.churchtimes.co.uk

title: Lady Margaret Hall
text: Type: organization

title: Lady Margaret Hall
text:
Description
Founded as a hall for women students in 1879, with Elizabeth Wordsworth as its Lady Principal, it became a college of Oxford University in 1959. See www.lmh.ox.ac.uk

title: Lady Margaret Hall
text:
Letters Mentioning This

Letter 3414

title: Children’s World, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Children’s World, The
text:
Description
This periodical was published by the Church Missionary Society (1895-1900).

title: Chambers’s Journal
text: Type: periodical

title: Chambers’s Journal
text:
Description
This journal was founded in 1832 as Chambers’ Edinburgh Journal and became in 1854 Chambers’s Journal of Popular Literature, Science and Arts, under which name it ran until 1897. CMY refers to it in No 49 and also Nos 56, 124, 127.

title: Aunt Judy’s Magazine
text: Type: periodical

title: Aunt Judy’s Magazine
text:
Description
This was a children’s magazine which ran 1866-1885. It was edited by Margaret Gatty until 1873; from 1874-6 by her daughters Horatia Gatty and Juliana Ewing; from 1877-85 by Horatia Gatty.

title: Monthly Packet, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Monthly Packet, The
text:
Description
This monthly magazine was edited by CMY 1851-1890; edited by CMY jointly with C. R. Coleridge 1891-3; edited by C. R. Coleridge and A. D. Innes 1894-9. It was markedly High Church and aimed at a juvenile audience, mainly conceived of as girls in their teens, though in the early days CMY also hoped to make it entertaining to boys who had not yet gone to public school, and there is evidence that it was also read by adults of both sexes. The magazine has been digitized in the series 19th Century UK Periodicals and is available at http://find.galegroup.com/ukpc/

title: Monthly Packet, The
text:

Letter 3409
