title: 3371_To_Beatrice_Henrietta_Heathcote.html
text:
’
My dear Beatrice Henrietta Heathcote, It must have been the Die and not the seal itself that was hidden in the wall. Charlotte Fortescue Yonge has discovered at the Croft Vertue’s Cromwell seals and medals with Richard’s seal - he is on horseback on one side and in parliament on the other. Also that Timbs in his Abbeys, Castles and Ancient halls of England and Walessays that ’Wllm Heathcote Esq found the die and sold it but Sir William Heathcote bought the die.[[footnote:1]] This is on the authority of Vertue in 1760, but as he thought it was the die of the seal of the Long Parliament.
I do not much hold to his authority, and if the seal or die was bought back to Hursley, what could have become of it?
I think the actual seal is a flat thing, like a coin (only concave) and the die big and solid more like a weight.
Anna Rachel Bramston has sent me the rubbing and it is gone to The Rev. Edmund Doidge Anderson Morshead. The Frances White who erected the monument to Sir Charles Wyndham must have been the wife of the man with the Crease
I have written to Tankerville Chamberlayne to ask after Sir Isaac Newton’s sun dial. I think - and so does Louisa Alethea (Yonge) Bowles that there used to be one at Cranbury on the slope above King’s Lane
Boyatt Lane and High bridge used to belong to Brambridge so I can get in the avenues being pollarded for gun stocks, and Mrs Fitzherbert whom my Frances Mary (Bargus) Yongesaw there and remembered her eyebrows
Yours affectionately C M Yonge

title: 3373_To_Catharine_Elgie.html
text:
’
My dear Catharine (Hammond) Elgie, later Elgee There is a poor old surgeon of 80 for whom I have been voting for [sic]. If he is no more I will vote for Annie Reynolds as I always think Winchester has the first claim
Caroline Ray, Charles Ray’s eldest sister, who has spent her life in nursing invalid old gentlemen, and been well paid, takes Price’s shop, as soon as his nephew can sell off the remains
Yours affly C M Yonge

title: 3375_To_Frederick_Orridge_Macmillan.html
text:
’
Dear Sir Frederick Orridge Macmillan Herewith I send the MS of ‘John Keble’s Parishes: A History of Hursley and Otterbourne. By Charlotte M. Yonge, An Old Inhabitant.’ I shall be glad to know what you think the best way of dealing with it and what you think about illustrations.
There should be a map of the parishes which are locked together but I wait to get it drawn out till I hear about the size of the page
What would be advisable as to illustrations. We had thought of
Ruins of Merdon Castle Old house at Hursley Cup and other relics found in a deep well Great Seal of Richard Cromwell Old Church of Hursley Otterbourne Church Ampfield Church Hursley Church
Portrait of Sir William Heathcote 5th Bt. & The Rev. John Keble
Would these be too expensive?
What would be the terms
If possible the book should be out by the middle of October, as a Jubilee of the Church’s Consecration is to be kept on that day/24th
Yours sincerely C M Yonge

title: 3377_To_Frederick_Orridge_Macmillan_or_Alexander_Macmillan_Jr.html
text:
’
Dear Sir Frederick Orridge Macmillan I hope to send off the illustrations - or the material for them on Saturday.[[footnote:2]]
I will write to Melville Portal, the Chairman of the Quarter Sessions for permission for the portrait of Sir William Heathcote 5th Bt.
Two books are with them - one (Selina (Shirley), Lady Heathcotes) for the sake of the old views and plans - it is the original Memoranda of the Parishes of Hursley and North Baddesley, in the County of Southampton.
The A Guide to the Village of Hursley, the Home of Keble, author of ‘The Christian Year’ belonging to me is the The Rev. John Frewen Moor jr.’s written about 1860.
The photographs are by a man named William Savage, who is dead I marked what are worth looking at also two little woodcuts The photo of the Hursley Vicarage is much better than that in the envelope, and more characteristic, but Frith’s may be better than either
I need hardly say that all the collection is valuable and we trust to their return in safety[[footnote:3]]
Yours truly C M Yonge
Some are sent to be chosen from

title: 3379_To_Frederick_Orridge_Macmillan.html
text:
’
Dear Sir Frederick Orridge Macmillan I am asking The Rev. John Medley (The Rev John Medley Tyntesfield, Bristol) to write to you direct about his Grandfather’s drawing.[[footnote:1]] It is rather a question what he may feel able to do, as it does not belong to him but to his cousin. It is in a book, but whether only fastened in, or bound up I cannot tell
I have told him that in either case, book or drawing would be quite safe with you. It is a very beautiful and characteristic sketch and should have John Bacon’s name.
Here is an addition to be sent to the printer. Selina (Shirley), Lady Heathcote (being past 80) could not find it at first and her daughters did not like to press the matter, I do not like notes encumbering pages but if the alteration to the text is too much this might go as a note at the end of the first chapter
I send the proofs through Helena Mary Heathcote so as to be secure from mistakes, as she is much more accurate than myself, besides having local knowledge on the Hursley side
Yours truly C M Yonge

title: 3381_To_the_Countess_of_Glasgow.html
text:
’
My dear Montagu (Abercromby) Boyle[[footnote:1]] That is a beautiful testimony from the Scotsman to the great work at Sta. Cruz, The Right Rev. Cecil Wilson is keeping it up, and now it is under British protection his work will be the less hindered. I believe the Church to be built in memory of The Rt. Rev. John Richardson Selwyn is to be in the island of Florida, where there is a considerable number of scholars. He says that everywhere the teaching of Christianity is called by even the heathen the preaching of Peace. Sometimes a village sends a deputation to ask for the Preaching of Peace, and really they do begin by giving up wars and deadly feuds—also by keeping the Sabbath, which last is so congenial to their laziness that they make two or three Sabbaths in a week.
Hursley is preparing for a great celebration of the 50th Anniversary of the Consecration of the Church on the 24th of October.
We are to hear of South Africa on Sunday week. No doubt you have seen the History of the Universities’ Mission to Central Africa 1859-1896, The By A.E.M. Anderson-Morshead. With a Preface by Charlotte Mary Yonge. which is just coming to a second edition.
I am afraid people think there is too much good advice in Mothers in Council, and want more variety. There is an interesting appeal for the deaf and dumb mothers coming in the next number.
Yours very sincerely C M Yonge

title: 3383_To_Anna_Meredith_Barrett_Lennard.html
text:
’
My dear Anna Meredith (Harris) Barrett Lennard I must send your name on a separate piece of paper, as the book must go from Macmillan and Son as I do not keep a stock of copies by me, and besides I think booksellers packing is safer than home, if I had a copy I was sure was new all through[[footnote:2]].
What a sad loss of all your precious books, that can never be repaired! We are looking forward to the 50th Consecration of Hursley church when there will be a great gathering and an octave[?] of services
We had a Te Deum for the victory[[footnote:3]] in church the Sunday after we heard of it. The Rev. Henry Albany Bowles jr. had three cousins there so it came very near. Today I have been reading of the grand reception in London
What a state the world is in - and is France ever to retrieve its general character, each generation falls lower than the last
Yours sincerely C M Yonge

title: 3386_To_Christabel_Rose_Coleridge.html
text:
’
My dear Christabel Rose Coleridge Do you want Popular Tales of the West Highlands? I dont think there is anything bearing on Arthur in them he was quite Cymric not Gaelic. I sent the two Magazine for the Young yesterday. Shall I write notices of Society for the Promotion of Christian Knowledge, The’s books? They are not a good lot thus far as I have read, and there are two by Miss E Finnimore, The Postwoman and Uncle Isaac's Money that I am very angry with, they are about farmer folk of the rough cast sort, but with no token of principle or religion, true enough to nature unluckily, but not good to put out with SPCK stamp. I have written to Daniel Conner Lathbury to ask leave to say my say in the Guardian, The. Phoebe Anne Allen’s May-Duke Blossoms: A story for Mothers' Meetings gets into a complication of last fathers and sons not so good as last years and FE Beach who generally writes well, has made Ash Wednesday come after the 1st Sunday in Lent There must be a terrible want of censorship in the whole concern. We had a great Girls’ Friendly Society, The course of sermon and meeting last week, and the Mothers’ Union, The comes in tomorrow and next day - Then the Hursley anniversary on Monday for which some of my people are coming, I lodging the women folk, Marion Finlaison the men, having only too much room Very anxious to know what becomes of Preston and Mrs Jeune. I am very glad Frances Sophia Coleridge Patteson is coming to poor Sophia (Coleridge) Martyn
The Rev. Canon Edgar Wharton preaches such a nice sermon on unconscious influence putting me so in mind of what has come round to me of Frances Mary (Bargus) Yonge’s effect on people, and bearing out my view that it is unconscious
I have written my Xmas GFS letter, it is rather stupid on the allegory of the voyage of life. Miss Knight wants a New Years letter for GL - I mean to do the Century of Victory & progress with drawbacks
your affte C M Yonge
Do you get National Society, The books?

title: 3388_To_Christabel_Rose_Coleridge.html
text:
’
My dear Christabel Rose Coleridge Poor dear Sophia (Coleridge) Martyn, she has been a heavy weight on many minds from the time of Mary Sophia Paulina Martyn’s death, in a remarkable way considering the clever, able woman she was.[[footnote:1]] I heard of her release, for such it was from Mary Yonge who wanted much, as well as Charlotte Cordelia (Pode) Yonge to come to the funeral, but happily the two witheld each other, in the fogs and the rain and the wet grass, so it was a very good thing they prevented each other-
I am glad Frances Sophia Coleridge Patteson was there. You have a splendid possession, according to this advt. Mine is only seven volumes. Here is an entertaining letter from Margaret; what storms!
I think SS Agnes and Margaret were rather RC saints for Irish to swallow and I would eliminate Hope Carlyon if possible She seems to me in SPCK to be of a very low order of sense.[[footnote:2]] The 'Elf: The Christmas Number Prize Story' is a very beautiful idea, though practically I doubt a birth deformed person being so amiable, I think the temper is generally astray and no wonder. Yes, it is a good number for Christmas. 'La Belle Louise' is a charming story, and the 'A Household Emergency' capital- the others, even Frances Mary Peard’s, very fair commonplace- but it is very good altogether.[[footnote:3]] Cupid is getting better, but had not been dressed when last we heard of him, it was really a sharp attack, and it was well he had his Granny.
I have written a thing for the G L, on the century and am asked to write one for the Girls' Realm, The
‘A Girl’s Experiment’ Religious Tract Society, The is really good for either degree of girl. The Whipping-Boy Wells Gardner is also good. Mary Eliza Bramston thinks it possible for Elementary Girls' Friendly Society Reading Union
your affte C M Yonge

title: 3390_To_an_Unknown_Person.html
text:
’
Have you heard the last variety of American Lion hunting – writing to ask for a bit of one’s dress to put into a literary quilt? I actually had such a request, and while I was laughing at it I came on a book of Louisa May Alcott’s where the dear Jo – after she had become a noted authoress – has the same entreaty made to her.

title: 3392_To_the_Reverend_John_Frederick_Watkinson_Bullock.html
text:
’
Dear The Rev. John Frederick Watkinson Bullock, I am glad you have succeeded at last in getting an opportunity for publishing[[footnote:1]]
Certainly executors are very troublesome to deal with as they are not acting for themselves. I am sending an order paper for one person, a cousin, who I hope may have one
Yours truly C M Yonge

title: 3394_To_Sir_Walter_Besant.html
text:
’
Dear Sir Walter Besant I have been so much interested by the book you have kindly sent me, in common with rest of the Society of Authors, The and, having had a little correspondence with you many years ago, when you were editing the English Plutarch, I venture to write, thinking you may care to hear some experiences of a long life of writing, not from necessity but because I had something to say.
The passion for telling a story developed itself early, but began to take shape after reading Alisons History of Europe during the French Revolution. (History of Europe from the commencement of the French Revolution … to the Restoration of the Bourbons.) from Moscow. William Crawley Yonge was an exact and fastidious man and he criticised and condemned every awkward expression in my MS till I could not get on, and it was laid aside for some years. Then the desire to make the beauty of Manzoni’s I promessi sposi: storia milanese del secolo XVII known to him led to my translating it - but as another translation came out, the only benefit was the training in language, and when I next attempted original composition, it passed muster though much corrected.
That was my training in style, except the knowing his great admiration for the scene of Guy Mannering, or, The Astrologer, and Old Mortalityto his home. When people ask me how to form a style, I always tell them ‘Take care of your grammar and your style will take care of itself’ - Though I do not know whether this is right, and of course there is an arrangement of syllables necessary -
Nothing taught me so much as to the keeping to a story and the subordination of details as Sir Joshua Reynold’s old  Seven Discourses delivered in the Royal Academy by the President.
Conversations benefitted by the habit of early youth, in gaieté du coeur of writing down in dramatic fashion the actual talks of a large merry party and I should recommend the habit as giving life to dialogue.
Being a strong and practical man, my Father looked into publishing accounts, and came to most of your conclusions. ‘Little Duke, or, Richard the Fearless, The. By the author of “The Heir of Redclyffe”, “The Kings of England.”’ was actually printed at Winchester and published on commission but my Father died suddenly while I was still very young, and being no accountant I was glad to leave things in the hands of John William Parker jr. to whom Sir John Taylor Coleridge had recommended me for the Heir of Redclyffe, The By the author of “The Two Guardians,” “Henrietta’s Wish,” “The Kings of England.” 2 vols., 1750 first Edition half profits, on his giving up I passed to Macmillan and Son and I do not think I have had any cause to complain. Curiously however the Daisy Chain, or, Aspirations: A Family Chronicle, The. By the author of “The Heir of Redclyffe.” 2 Vols, though brought out in awkward form, and never advantageously received has in the long run been more successful than any book, though Heartsease, or, The Brother’s Wife. 2 vols. By the author of “The Heir of Redclyffe.” reached 2000 the first year.
My books for poor children remained with Mozley for whom I edited the Monthly Packet, The, and for his successors, for more than 30 years, with fair success in its own field, juvenile (not children’s) and religious. Thus I have had experience in editorship enough to go along with what you say as to the hopelessness of the poor people who begin to write for a purpose - Also in general of recommendations. Only one of mine succeeded that of Margaret Eliza Roberts, who would have done as well without it.
One other curious fact I may mention. ‘The Heroes of Asgard and the Giants of Jötunheim, or, the Week and its Story’ was sent me from the Guardian, The with a large parcel of other books. The small type, the awkward form and generally local or provincial appearance made me suppose it was a strictly private or provincial sort - and left it to the last
Then of course I saw what a remarkable book it was. I Review of John Inglesant it there, and told others of it, especially The Rev. Richard St. John Tyrwhitt. After a time The Rt. Hon. William Ewart Gladstone took it up, and it was the book of the day, though Joseph Henry Shorthouse had tried in vain to publish it and did so at his own expense.
I sometimes wonder if it lives. He has done nothing to equal it.
And - if you are not tired of this long letter, I should like to add that often in first efforts there is a charm of freshness and spontaneity not to be found in later work, though that may be really superior and better in workmanship. Peter Simple, The Confessions of Harry Lorrequer, Waverley, or, 'Tis Sixty Years Since, perhaps The Posthumous Papers of the Pickwick Club, Alice's Adventures in Wonderland, Reata: What's in a Name? are all specimens of what I mean. One feels the enjoyment of the story-teller before he has to work up to his former performance.
Begging you to excuse me for this long egotistical letter
Yours sincerely C M Yonge
The Society has twice successfully interfered on my behalf[[footnote:2]]

title: 3396_To_Mary_Elizabeth_Sumner.html
text:
’
My dear Mary Elizabeth (Heywood) Sumner I hope you had the bundle of materials for the April Mothers in Council that I sent to Claremont, Rudleston, Manchester on the 31st January, for it is time Wells, Gardner had them. If there is nothing to alter, perhaps you would leave them at his office, writing Mothers in Council on them. I only want to add a most excellent book of The Sunday School Institute Christ and the Catechism: The Church Catechism by 520 questions and answers explained but that can be done in the proofs.
Here is Mrs Bartram, Ramsgate Vicarage writing to ask leave for their Organist to set to music ‘God Knows’ out of our October number - I suppose there is no objection, but I do not now remember where it came from.
I shall wait to answer till I hear from you.
I have not had Mrs Guslett’s paper
Yours affectionately C M Yonge

title: 3398_To_Frederick_Orridge_Macmillan.html
text:
’
Dear Sir Frederick Orridge Macmillan I believe I wrote before and asked whether you thought it worth while to revive two or three books of mine that have gone out of print. I have been asked about them again, which leads me to write. ‘Two Guardians; or, Home in this World, The By the author of “Henrietta’s Wish,” “Kenneth,” etc.’ and ’Henrietta’s Wish, or, Domineering: A Tale. By the author of “Scenes and Characters”.  were published by Masters and went through three editions at least, then I heard no more of them and his business has been given up. The copyright is mine.
They are both ‘stories for young people’ and were a good deal liked and honestly I think there is good work in them.
Kenneth, or, The Rear Guard of the Grand Army. By the author of “Scenes and Characters,” “Kings of England,” etc. was published by John Henry Parker. I believe he has let it drop, but there must be some merit in it since Mr E Arnold wrote to ask permission to make extracts in a Reading book
I thank you much for the beautiful series on Natural History. The birds delight me much
Yours very truly C M Yonge

title: 3400_To_Frederick_Orridge_Macmillan.html
text:
’
Dear Sir Frederick Orridge Macmillan I am glad you see your way to publishing these books on the half profit system I send the Two Guardians; or, Home in this World, The By the author of “Henrietta’s Wish,” “Kenneth,” etc. and Henrietta’s Wish, or, Domineering: A Tale. By the author of “Scenes and Characters”.  shall follow, as soon as we can supply the first two pages which are gone from the only copy I can find
Yours truly C M Yonge

title: 3402_To_the_Marchioness_of_Salisbury.html
text:
’
My dear Georgina Caroline (Alderson) Gascoyne-Cecil I am venturing to ask if you would be so good as to present my niece Joanna Angela (Yonge) Cromie at the coming drawing room. Her husband Captain Charles Francis Cromie has just been promoted from Morocco to Algeciras, and they are in England during the transit. It would of course be much more convenient to her to be presented now that she is at home than to have to come on purpose.
If you have not to present her officially[[footnote:1]] would you do so unofficially? And can you let her know at once that she may send in her name for the 10th or 16th of May.
Her address is 6 Cornwall Terrace Regents Park NW
I hope you are stronger[[footnote:2]] and have escaped this spell of influenza
yours sincerely C M Yonge

title: 3404_To_Mary_Yonge.html
text:
’
My dear Mary Yonge We had two plants of purple periwinkle once in the old shrubbery part of the garden. It disappeared, and I tried to introduce it here from Dogmersfield, where there was plenty, but it did not live. Tell Jenny the bar is a line or stripe going horizontally all across the shield [diagram] The wreath is supposed to be the folds or fastening on the top of the helmet on which the crest is fixed and often it is made to bend a little to follow the shape of the head [diagram] [[footnote:1]]
I never heard that the livery was bound to follow it and I don’t think old George Smith had any red in his. Perhaps Miss Duke brought it in with the blue, but then it would have been blue and white[[footnote:2]]
George Alan [or Allan?] David Yonge was in the Matabele war, I believe his fort was beyond Bulawayo, but he found that the police are disbanded, rode to Salisbury to see Sir Richard Edward Rowley Martin who promised to find him employment if he came out again. [[footnote:3]] That was on Christmas Eve, when he swam a river and had to lie in bed till his clothes were dried. After that he had a fever in a Portuguese hospital very dirty and where nothing could be done for you without pay, not even anything to eat.
[part of letter removed with signature]
next morning
Fraulein Fraulein Marie Heller Schäpers Strasse Berlin
Marion Finlaison knows that this is a very good person, a friend of Frances Sophia Coleridge Pattesons who wants several English to teach for an hour a day, to pay £25 as premium but to be at no further expense.
You don’t say where Charlotte Cordelia (Pode) Yonge is going I suppose
[rest of letter missing with signature]

title: 3406_To_Lady_Leconfield.html
text:
’Dear Constance Evelyn (Primrose) Wyndham[[footnote:1]] Indeed I enjoyed your visit with Mary Josephine Yonge Harris very much. She is an old friend connected with happy old days at Torquay, and the shyness that ought not to hang about one at seventy five is not a thing that endures when one well known comes at the same time. If ever you are near enough again, I hope to see you if you think it worth while - and I shall be happy to write in your birthday book. Your allowance of the Daisy Chain, or, Aspirations: A Family Chronicle, The. By the author of “The Heir of Redclyffe.” 2 Vols reminds me of my chapter a day of Sir Walter Scott Bt., though I don’t think one of my chapters could have been half so well worth reading I think however they were longer, and might make up in quantity for what was wanting in quality- I used to go back to the beginning and read up the previous chapters of Waverley, or, 'Tis Sixty Years Since &c over and over up to my days allowance, and I had always to take a previous dose of The Roman History from the Foundation of the city of Rome to the Destruction of the western Empire, where I greatly preferred chapters about the wickedest Emperors they were so much the most entertaining
yours sincerely C M Yonge

title: 3408_To_Mary_Yonge.html
text:
’
My dear Mary Yonge I am glad you have been keeping the wedding day with Charlotte Cordelia (Pode) Yonge.[[footnote:1]] I am afraid that the hotel must spoil sitting out of doors except in front of the house. I am writing now in the garden while Blanche C. Webber, who is here to recover from the remains of the influenza is lying down in her own room. She had it at Easter and does not quite get over the remains, so she is come to breathe her native air here, till Major Robert Woollcombe RMA and Mary (Walter) Woollcombe come on the 22d. I believe they go to Dorking today. I think it is rather cruel of Baldwin John Pollexfen Bastard to forbid the parties from Plymouth as they do not molest him.[[footnote:2]] I was asked the other day if Kitley was a desirable place for a young laundry maid Girls’ Friendly Society, The. I could not say that I thought I should choose it. I had a nice letter today from one of our Dr. Barnardo's Homes girls who was sent to Canada five years ago. She has always been in the same place, and has had two prizes from her Sunday school at Toronto, where she was pleased to find Yonge Street.[[footnote:3]] She had been on an expedition in a steamer to see Niagara, but they only had ten minutes there – rather American like the man who said enough of Lago Maggiore in one flash of lightning- My budlea[sic] is looking very pretty. No one but Devonshire people ever seem to have seen one. My guests always ask what it is. I kept The Rev. Charles Burell Yonge’s wedding at a garden party at the The Rt. Rev. George Henry Sumner’s[[footnote:4]], where among many other people I met Mrs Spender[[footnote:5]], who was pleased to hear about the wedding. Edward Harold Spender has written an interesting Through the High Pyrenees about the Pyrenees, starting from the Mediterranean, which I never heard about before. I am so glad Charles did not consent to the display of wedding presents, I always dislike the notices so much, but then as The Rev. George Rundle Prynnesaid when his father and mother were married ‘The Yonges are not demonstrative’ Poor old Mr Prynne, he and The Rev. Canon Thomas Thellusson Carter are nearly the only survivors of the old days.[[footnote:6]] Canon Carter still preaches, though he is 90. We have had scattered up and down the village a tract with a regular attack on the The Rt. Rev. Edward White Benson, called ‘The Real Presence’ evidently written by an actual NonConformist. Scraps of it have been picked up along the road so some at least must have been torn up. The Rev. Henry Albany Bowles jr. goes to Switzerland on the 17th of July, and Louisa Alethea (Yonge) Bowles and the children to Holmwood close to Dorking. Joanna Angela (Yonge) Cromie’s two little boys sail with their nurse on the 26th. She has been to a ball at Gibraltar and it will be much pleasanter than Morocco.
Our English Church Union, The festival is to be at Cranbury on the 21th. If the weather holds it will be beautiful but rain is wanted, though not by the hay which is almost got in. Charlotte Cordelia (Pode) Yonge must be enjoying the garden at Slade, which The Rev. Edmund Doidge Anderson Morshead and I agreed was the most beautiful we knew. Edmund and Mary (Fearon) Anderson Morshead called the other day
your most affec C M Yonge

title: 3410_To_Margaret_Jane_Mowbray.html
text:
’
My dear Margaret Jane Mowbray- I am afraid I did not thank you or any one else for all your kindness to me. I had no notion of all that the function involved, and I fear I have never outgrown ungracious shyness, which I am often sorry for, and I am afraid stood in the way in the many introductions. [[footnote:1]] But nothing could have been better managed or more gratifying than the whole, and I can only thank you and your staff and your white band of maidens for one of the prettiest and pleasantest recollections of a lifetime.-
Yours sincerely, C. M. YONGE.

title: 3412_To_Christabel_Rose_Coleridge.html
text:
’
My dear Christabel Rose Coleridge If I were to be dissected while I am alive, I think you would do it tenderly, but indeed I have always shrunk from seeing the lives of living people and my whole old fashioned nature revolts at the idea partly personally, and partly because I know how those who are gone would feel about it, so indeed I do not think it is possible to my feelings and I hope you will not be vexed.
We are overwhelmed with applications for the Infant school at Allbrook, having advertised in the Schoolmistress, The. One offers herself from Stirling! She would be odd here I think. We have Mary Elizabeth (Heywood) Sumner’s presentation next week, all the mothers in the diocese to drink tea at Wolvesey, and the week after our bazaar for the schools- after which from the 3d to the 6th, I go down to the Heathcotes at Beechwood and on the 10th Charlotte Anne Elizabeth Moberly comes to me for ten days, and I shall get a dip in Oxford news. Mr The Rev. William Cosmo Gordon Lang made a splendid speech at the Winchester Diocesan Society for Higher Religious Education, The meeting.
Arthur Donald Innes’s promises dont seem to come to anything only he says his company won’t let him get at the money to pay his debts till all is paid in.
I wonder if it would do any good to send a lawyer to call on him or if it would be only sending good money after bad.
He has given no account for my own books, as well as /contributions to\ the Monthly Packet, The. If you are not paid for all this past year, I wonder you do not strike but he keeps on pacifying you.
You never mentioned Alice.
your affectionate C M Yonge

title: 3414_To_Elizabeth_Harcourt_Mitchell.html
text:
’
My dear Elizabeth Harcourt (Rolls) Mitchell Thank you for your ‘Education and Manners: A Trialogue’.[[footnote:1]] It reminds me of what I tried to impress on some of the promoters of Lady Margaret Hall, that the Old Colleges began with training for the church the first object, and the secular work a sort of appendage, the Christian training running through. And I tried to shadow it out in that drawing of Geraldine's in the Pillars of the House, or, Under Wode, Under Rode, The. 4 vols., of the Christian School of Athens. If you happen to have the book you will see the ideal.
I think the Talbots would have been glad to have such a college[[footnote:2]], but times are too strong, and Elizabeth Wordsworth and Charlotte Anne Elizabeth Moberly at St. Hugh’s College do make their colleges in many respects training for the Church.
Yours sincerely C. M. Yonge

title: 3416_To_Christabel_Rose_Coleridge.html
text:
’
My dear Christabel Rose Coleridge I am glad there are some signs of life or of decay more properly of poor Arthur Donald Innes- ! I do think there are more and more signs that a 6d phoenix would be welcomed.[[footnote:1]]
I find the booksellers make all sorts of excuses for not having got it – The Society of Authors, The want to see my agreement before advising me. I could not find it easily before I left home and was too hot for a hunt. Frances Mary Peard has written to offer a visit on the 17th or 18th so I have written to her to meet me and come with me on the 18th I have got the MS of Modern Broods, or, Developments Unlooked For. , I think I can make them much more interesting and longer. We are nice and cool here now, but I think people must suppose that I dispose of all the Charlotte Yonge Scholarship, The money for the begging letters are endless. Anna Rachel Bramston has photographed all the tableaux before the break up! Poor George Alan [or Allan?] David Yonge at Kimberley is tormented as nephew to a lion, and was invited to go to a book dance as ‘Daisy Chain, or, Aspirations: A Family Chronicle, The. By the author of “The Heir of Redclyffe.” 2 Vols’- pretty well for a great black police officer. So you may tell Walter Henry Praed Coleridge to prepare to be invited out as Jack a Lantern. Helen Emma Yonge returns remembrances. I have had a charming letter from the old Mary Frances Younghusband about Maximilian and the Martinswand- and an Italian palace – quite good enough for an article. [[footnote:1]] The local demon The Rev. M.M. ffinch & co are a great success, he reads the lessons so beautifully that I am quite sorry to miss a fortnight of him. He is warden of a college for ‘decayed gentlewomen’ I should think you would have heard of it.
Maurice Edward Yonge and Maude (Woods) Yonge have the funniest little cottage at Fleet, in the middle of purple heath
your affectionate C M Yonge
Poor Lanty!

title: 3418_To_Christabel_Rose_Coleridge.html
text:
’
My dear Christabel Rose Coleridge I am sure unless we could get an amount of subscribers enough to start a new Church Monthly Packet, The with security, it would be vain. Could we find out? Otherwise Sir Walter Besant is right, and we must acquiesce. I would make Modern Broods, or, Developments Unlooked For.  a fresh start for it is needed, but I think it is not hopeful, sorry as I am, especially that Church should be a decided drawback.[[footnote:2]] If it eschewed Controversy Churchbuilding charity and saints I should think it might get on But men such as The Rev. Canon Arthur Rawson Ashwell are hard to catch in these days Frances Mary Peard has changed her mind and says she won’t come, but is going to India . I have written to beg her to meet me at Waterloo and come home with me on Friday but I don’t know if she will.
I have excogitated a new end for Ducks Eggs working in the old friends. All were going out to Carrigaboola, via San Frisco- Magdalen- Paula and all, with Angela and the Bishop – also Bernard who – on holiday in England was suddenly wanted for a Travis & Underwood emergency, involving California Ship either wrecked or on fire not far beyond Liverpool (Charlotte Anne Elizabeth Moberly wanted a woman to hide for the sake of saving a more valuable life of Man) Angela dies – putting her Sisters bonnet on someone else. All the women are saved, but Bernard is struck down by a spar left unseen, insensible He comes to himself to find Angela hanging over him, the golden curls looking like her childhood. They think they are going together, but a Ewemouth collier picks them up in time- Bernard lifted into unconscious but Angela falls in lifting him into the sea
Phyllis had had to be left at home- So you have the account in Bernard’s words to Clement
your affte C M Yonge

title: Organizations
text:
‘’Bethlehem’’
70th Birthday Presentation to CMY
 Charm, The
 Church Quarterly Review, The
 Church Times, The
Academy, The
All Saints Magazine
Atalanta
Athenaeum, The
Aunt Judy’s Magazine
Barnacle, The
Blackwood’’s Edinburgh Magazine
British Quarterly Review, The
Cassell’s Magazine
Chambers’s Journal
Children’s World, The
Christian Remembrancer, The
Church Illustrated, The
Churchman’’s Companion
Churchman’’s Family Magazine, The
Churchwoman, The
Constitutional Press
Cornhill Magazine, The

title: Otterbourne Penny Club
text: Type: organization

title: Otterbourne Penny Club
text:
Description
Mentioned in letter no 42. CMY mentions in Old Times at Otterbournethat a lending library for poor people was also run through the Penny Club with books originally given in memory of James Yonge by his grieving father the Rev. John Yonge: ‘In 1834 their father gave what made, as it were the second foundation of the Lending Library, for there were about four-and-twenty very serious books, given in Archdeacon Heathcote’s time, kept in the vestry at the old Church.  They looked as if they had been read but only by the elder people who liked a grave book, and there was nothing there meant for the young people.  So there were a good many new books bought, and weekly given out at the Penny Club, with more or less vigour, for the next thirty years or so.’ The Club was evidently still going in 1870, when Mary Yonge seems to have taken over her sister Anne’s subscription of 2/2.

title: Marriage between first cousins
text: Type: topic

title: Marriage between first cousins
text:
Description
CMY was against this, and such marriages are not encouraged in any of her novels, although they are a normal feature of nineteenth-century fiction by other writers (occurring for example without disapproval in Mansfield Park and Middlemarch), just as they were a commonplace feature of Victorian life. The topic is mentioned (15, 20 March 1860) in letters to Ann Maria Carter Smith and discussed by CMY in ‘Three Generations’ Mothers in Council 5.17 (Jan 1895). Her opinions were probably formed because of her family history. In her autobiographical writing she observes of her grandmother’s family ‘the Crawleys were far too much addicted to marriages among cousins’. This is in connection with the marriage of her uncle James Yonge to his first cousin Margaret Crawley; his sisters Catharina and Charlotte also married Margaret’s brothers Charles and George. James Yonge was a physician in Plymouth, His three children all died within a year in 1830. There was a growing scientific evidence against the excessive inbreeding, reinforced for the agriculture-minded landed gentry by the lessons drawn from experience with horses, dogs and farm animals. Moreover there was also much discussion in religious circles of the prohibited degrees. Pusey, giving evidence in 1847 to the Commission appointed to enquire into the state and operation of the law of marriage (which was much preoccupied with the deceased wife’s sister issue), cited St. Augustine’s view that the marriage of first cousins, though lawful, was near to incest (Parliamentary Papers 1848, 40).

title: Auckland Bells, The

title: Auckland Bells, The
text:
Description
This was an eventually successful campaign to raise money so that the sound of church bells should be heard in New Zealand: see MP (July 1857- February 1863).

title: higher education of women, The
text: Type: topic

title: Otterbourne School
text: Type: organization

title: Otterbourne School
text:
Description
This institution, now Otterbourne Church of England Primary School, has its origins in the school founded by CMY’s mother in about 1826 ‘the room built of cement, with two tiny ones behind for kitchen and bed- room for the mistress, and a brick floor’ (John Keble’s Parishes, 95). The schoolmistress, however, could not keep the boys in order, and so a separate boys’ room was built near the church. In 1838, when Otterbourne church was rebuilt on a new site, a boys’ school was ‘built on a corner of the ground intended as churchyard, and a larger room added to the girls', the expense being partly defrayed by a bazaar held at Winchester, and in part by Charlotte Yonge's first book’ (John Keble’s Parishes, 103). The National Society was also involved, and the school was sometimes called Otterbourne National School. In 1871 a new girls’ school and a master’s house was built to commemorate the service of the Rev. William Bigg Wither, curate of Otterbourne, and the school was moved ‘under government’ (Coleridge, Life, 263). The school’s archives 1872-1979 are held by Hampshire Archives. A pamphlet by Lloyd Woodcock, Charlotte Yonge and Otterbourne Girls' and Infants' School, was published by Eastleigh and District Local History Society in 1987. For the school website see http://www.otterbourneprimaryschool.com/

title: Spider Society, The
text: Type: organization

title: Spider Society, The
text:
Description
In January 1873 the last page of the Monthly Packet included an announcement:
TO OUR READERS
COBWEBS OR, THE MONTHLY PACKET ESSAY SOCIETY.
Two Questions, to be Answered in Writing, on History, Literature, Art, or Science, will be proposed in each Number. Answers to be directed to ARACHNE, CARE OF THE EDITOR OF THE MONTHLY PACKET. If 200 of our Subscribers will send a Shilling’s worth of Postage Stamps to the above Address, we will print the best one of the Answers to each Question, provided it does not exceed 2000 words, at the end of the NOTICES TO CORRESPONDENTS in the Number for the second month after the Questions come out. We do not offer a prize, as it is impossible to fix any limit as to age; but we hope sometimes to give a few words of help or criticism.

SPIDER QUESTIONS
Give an account of the history and derivation of the words – Vaunt, Brag, Bombast, and Rhodomontade.
Describe an Oak-leaf – its growth, structure, and historical and poetical associations.
Essay societies of this kind, whether associated with a magazine or not, were tremendously popular at this period. The background to Yonge’s foundation of the Cobwebs was her experience of running the Gosling Society between 1859 and 1877. The MP Christmas number for 1872 had consisted (as Christmas numbers often did in those days) of a series of stories by divers hands with a link story. In this case, the link story was the story of an essay society called ‘the Spiders’ ‘in graceful allusion to our being a fraternity of spinsters’. The author of this story was perhaps Christabel Coleridge. It gives an account, in fictional form, of a society which is plainly based on the Goslings. The foundation, immediately afterwards, of the new Cobweb Society was undoubtedly moving what had been a private organization into a more public sphere. When the Goslings were finally wound up in 1877 (after some years in which Yonge had been complaining about the quality of the work produced by recent members) the explanation which was given was that:
Having begun with 12 intimate friends & cousins who actually did 4 questions once a month it has come to only two once in two months & of those generally only one answered by a few - while there is little acquaintance between many[.] It seems therefore time to break it up, since there are other essay societies in plenty now and Mother Goose still superintends the Spiders.
The Spider essay society ran in MP until 1887, when it was wound up and CMY published a valedictory poem [[cmybook:274]'Arachne's Farewell'] in the April issue.

title: Santa Cruz Islands
text: Type: place

title: Santa Cruz Islands
text:
Description
The island of Nakapu was the scene of the death of the Rt. Rev. John Coleridge Patteson in 1871. It is an outlying island in the Reef (or Swallow) group of islands, which is in turn part of the group known as the Santa Cruz islands. The Santa Cruz islands form politically the province of Temotu in the Solomon Islands. The latter were declared a British protectorate in 1893 and achieved independence in 1978 as a constitutional monarchy with Queen Elizabeth II as head of state.

title: Ampfield
text: Type: place

title: Ampfield
text:
Description
Ampfield is a village in Hursley parish. At the instigation of Sir William Heathcote and the Rev. John Keble a new church, St. Mark, Ampfield, was built there, opened in 1841. It was on a slope in a pinewood, which struck them as very beautiful.

title: Friendly Work later the Girls’ Quarterly
text: Type: periodical

title: Friendly Work later the Girls’ Quarterly
text:
Description
This was a quarterly magazine published by the Girls' Friendly Society 1883-1917. It changed its name to the Girls' Quarterly in December 1894. It was edited initially by Mary Elizabeth Townsend, from 1893 by Louisa Fanny Field and from 1900 by Henley I. Arden (i.e. Henrietta Knight). It was aimed at Members of GFS who were better educated than those who read the sister periodical Friendly Leaves.

title: Keble’s funeral
text: Type: topic

title: Keble’s funeral
text:
Description
Keble's funeral, which CMY described as a 'gathering of the good' took place on Friday 6 April 1866 at the church of All Saints, Hursley. [[person:795]Charlotte Keble] was not present as she was lying dangerously ill in Bournemouth.
A large congregation is recorded in the accounts in John Bull (7 April 1866) 234 and the Pall Mall Gazette (7 April 1866) 8. The six former curates who carried the coffin were the Rev. Peter Young, the Rev. William Bigg Wither, the Rev. Robert Francis Wilson, the Rev. C. Le Geyt, the Rev. F. C. Alderson and the Rev. S. Scroggs. The Bishop of Salisbury (Walter Kerr Hamilton) was the only English bishop present, illustrating the suspicion in which the Tractarians were still held in many circles, though Bishop Forbes of Brechin was there. Cathedral clergy who did come included the Very Rev. Dr Hook, the Dean of Chichester, the Very Rev. Thomas Garnier, the aged Dean of Winchester, and the Ven. Philip Jacob, the Archdeacon of Winchester. Sir William Heathcote was there with his son-in-law Thomas Cooke Trench, as were Earl Nelson, Sir Frederic Rogers, General Wilbraham, Robert Brett, the Hon. Colin Lindsay and William Butterfield. Other well-known Tractarian clergy included the Rev. W. J. Butler, the Rev. R. W. Church, the Rev. C. F. Lowder, Dr. Pusey, Dr. Moberly, the Ven. Sir George Prevost, the Rev. William Upton Richards and the Rev. Henry Parry Liddon,

title: Winnington Hall School
text: Type: organization

title: Winnington Hall School
text:
Description
This girls' school was run by Margaret Alexis Bell, a friend of John Ruskin, who took a great interest in it.

title: Church of the Resurrection, Eastleigh
text: Type: place

title: Church of the Resurrection, Eastleigh
text:
Description
Built in 1868 with a donation of £500 from CMY. The architect was G. E. Street. It was destroyed by fire in 1985 and reconstructed as social housing flats in 2003.
See Tim Lambert, A Brief History of Eastleigh, Hampshire at www.localhistories.org/eastleigh
The Hampshire Advertiser (15 August 1868), 3, noted:
The consecration of the Church of the Resurrection, Eastliegh, took place on Thursday, by the Bishop of Mauritius. The church has been erected principally by the liberality of Miss Yonge, of Otterbourne, and Mr. - Chamberlaine, of Eastleigh Parish, together with other contributors. The Rev. W. A. Longlands, of Eling, near Southampton, has been appointed incumbent. A choir of amateurs has been trained by Mr. Somerford, of Winchester Cathedral. . . The services were intoned, or read, by the Rev. W. A. Longlands, incumbent, the Rev. W. Harrison, Vicar, Eastmeon, the Ven. Archdeacon Jacob, of Winchester, the Rev. R. F. Wilson, &c.

title: Church Missionary Society, The
text: Type: organization

title: Church Missionary Society, The
text:
Description
This was an important missionary society, founded in 1799, whose supporters came from the Evangelical wing of the Church of England.
Its archives 1799-1959 are held by the University of Birmingham and have been partly published on microfilm. The organisation still exists and is called the Church Mission Society.

text: Type: organization

title: Community of St. John the Baptist, Clewer, The
text:
Description
In 1849 the Clewer House of Mercy was founded in Windsor by Mariquita Tennant, as a refuge for repentant prostitutes. In 1852 this work was taken on by Harriet Monsell and the Rev. Thomas Thellusson Carter, Rector of Clewer, who founded a group of Sisters of Mercy.
The organisation still exists as an Anglican sisterhood, and its website is http://www.gjm61.dial.pipex.com/
According to A.M. Allchin, The Silent Rebellion: Anglican Religious Communities 1845-1900. (London: SCM Press, 1958), it became 'one of the most characteristically Victorian and nineteenth-century of all the sisterhoods' undertaking as its primary work the restoration of 'fallen women'.

title: English Church Union, The
text: Type: organization

title: English Church Union, The
text:
Description
This organisation was founded in 1859 in protest at the government legislating on church matters. It supported many of those accused of ritualism under the Public Worship Regulation Act (1874). The organisation changed its name to the Church Union in 1933, and still exists as a High Church lobby group within the Church of England, currently (2009) resisting the arrival of women bishops. Some archives are held by Lambeth Palace Library. Its website is http://www.churchunion.co.uk/

title: Books for the Chicago Exhibition
text: Type: topic

title: Books for the Chicago Exhibition
text:
Description
[[person:572]Alice Gordon] had written to the Editor of The Times (5 January 1893): 'A library has been built in the Women's Building of the Chicago Exhibition, in which the best books written by women of all nations are to be exhibited. We are anxious that English women's writings should be well represented. A committee has been formed consisting of Miss Agnes Clerke, Mrs. W. K. Clifford, Mrs. Green, Miss Kingsley, Mrs. Humphry Ward, Miss Charlotte Yonge, and Mrs. J. E. H. Gordon, to collect a representative library.' She asked for the gift or loan of books or MSS.

title: 70th Birthday Presentation to CMY
text: Type: topic

title: 70th Birthday Presentation to CMY
text:
Description
The presentation of an autograph album to CMY on her seventieth birthday, which was organized by the Moberly family, is described by Coleridge, [[otherbook:40]Life], 281-2, and Battiscombe, [[otherbook:17]Charlotte Mary Yonge], 159.
The following report appeared in The Times (16 August 1893), 8:
'The 70th birthday of Miss Yonge has been marked by a presentation to her from admirers in all parts of the world. An album containing 5.000 autographs and criticisms of her writings was left on her birthday at her dwelling in the village of Otterbourne. On the front page is the following inscription in an illuminated border:- \“Charlotte Mary Yonge - We offer our hearty congratulations on your 70th birthday, and desire to express to you the great enjoyment we have received from your writings, and our belief that they have done much good in this generation. August 11, 1893.\” Amongst the signatures are those of the Archbishop of York, the Earl of Selborne, the Marquis of Salisbury, Viscount Wolmer, the Bishops of London, Manchester, Salisbury, Chester, Bath and Wells, Chichester, Leicester, Reading, Southwell, Cape Town, Connecticut, and St. Helena, Bishops Selwyn, Jenner and Hobhouse, the Deans of Winchester, Windsor and Salisbury, Canon Scott Holland, the Warden of Keble College, Mr. Balfour and several members of Mr. Gladstone's family, besides the local clergy and gentry. The Queen of Italy sent a large photograph of herself, bearing her autograph and accompanied by a congratulatory note. Local presentations were made to Miss Yonge on the eve of her birthday.'

He was a nephew of the Rev. William Bigg Wither, curate of Otterbourne, and he published a History of the foundation and of the chief incidents in the gradual establishment of St. Thomas' Home, Basingstoke, the Winchester Diocesan Penitentiary for Friendless and Fallen Women (Basingstoke: Jacob 1887).

title: Home for Incurable and Infirm Women, The
text: Type: organization

title: Home for Incurable and Infirm Women, The
text:
Description
Mentioned 26 April 1864. This was run by [[person:1427]Louisa Twining], and operated from 21 Great Ormond Street. Twining described its work in an article in MP (April 1864) 437-449; and further in a letter MP (August 1869) 205-6. The latter gives the number of inmates as 27.
Some correspondence of the Charity Organization Society with an outfit of this name, running a home for respectable women in reduced circumstances at Whetstone, is held by the London Metropolitan Archives.

title: All Saints’ Church, Hursley
text: Type: place

title: All Saints’ Church, Hursley
text:
Description
The parish church of Hursley was substantially rebuilt in 1847-8 to the designs of William Harrison, under the supervision of the Rev. John Keble, and partly funded from the profits of his bestselling volume of poetry [[otherbook:95]The Christian Year]. CMY describes the fittings of the interior and the symbolism of the stained glass in some detail in [[cmybook:247]John Keble's Parishes] (1898), 115-124. She emphasises that the work coincided with the Tractarian crisis following Newman's secession, so that the reconstruction was an act of faith at a time of great anxiety and doubt, while the movement was the target of vilification and suspicion. She enumerates the gifts made by Keble's friends and supporters to beautify the building, and no doubt they symbolize among other things the dependence placed on him at that time.

title: Cambridge University Press

title: Band of Mercy

title: Band of Mercy

title: St. John’s College, Hurstpierpoint
text: Type: organization

title: St. John’s College, Hurstpierpoint
text:
Description
This was one of the Woodard schools, founded to provide good cheap education for the middle classes. Alice Coleridge lived there with her sister and brother-in-law, the Rev. Edward Clarke Lowe, headmaster 1850-73. The school’s website is http://www.hppc.co.uk/index.asp

title: Great Ormond Street Hospital
text: Type: organization

title: Great Ormond Street Hospital
text:
Description
A children’s hospital was founded in Great Ormond Street in 1852. Individuals and groups could sponsor beds for sick children. See Nicholas Baldwin, ‘The History of GOSH: The Whole Story’ at http://www.ich.ucl.ac.uk/about_gosh/gosh_history/

title: Mothers’ Union, The

title: Mothers’ Union, The
text:
Description
This was founded by Mary Elizabeth Sumner in 1885. CMY became the first editor of Mothers in Council, the journal aimed at the upper-class ladies who supported it.

title: Religious Tract Society, The

title: Religious Tract Society, The
text:
Description
This was an evangelical charity founded in 1799 which published devotional works on a very large scale. Although CMY did not write for it herself many of her friends did. After mergers it is now known as the United Society for Christian Literature; its archives are held by SOAS and have been published on microfilm.

title: Royal Victoria Hospital, Netley, The
text: Type: organization

title: Royal Victoria Hospital, Netley, The
text:
Description
This was a military hospital founded in 1856. Its first governor was CMY’s friend Colonel [[person:1515]Richard Wilbraham]. It survived until 1978 and is now a country park.
See http://www3.hants.gov.uk/hampshire-countryside/rvcp/history-rvcp.htm
and Anne Summers, [[otherbook:580]'Ministering angels'].

title: Society for the Promotion of Christian Knowledge, The
text: Type: organization

title: Society for the Promotion of Christian Knowledge, The
text:
Description
This charity, founded to promote Christianity at home and abroad in 1698, has long had and still has a substantial publishing arm. CMY planned to get it to publish a series of county histories (3, 5 November 1860). Unlike many of her friends, she did not write for it. Its archives are held in Cambridge University Library. See http://www.spck.org.uk/about-spck/archives/

title: Universities Mission to Central Africa
text:

This was the charity, founded in 1858, which sent the Rt. Rev. Charles Frederick Mackenzie to the Zambezi. It merged with the SPG in 1961.

title: Society of Authors, The

title: Society of Authors, The
text:
Description
This professional society was founded by the novelist [[person:1652]Walter Besant] in 1884 to protect the rights of authors. It carries on this work today; see http://www.societyofauthors.org/about_the_society/index.html

title: Society of the Most Holy Trinity, The
text: Type: organization

title: Society of the Most Holy Trinity, The
text:
Description
This was the sisterhood founded by [[person:1294]Lydia Sellon] in Plymouth as the Devonport Sisters of Mercy, which took this name on merging in 1856 with the Park Village Sisterhood of the Holy Cross (founded 1845). They were accused in 1849 by the Devonport Telegraphof adopting popish practices, see ‘The Sisters of Mercy at Devonport, Inquiry by the Bishop of Exeter’, Daily News (17 February 1849).

title: Keble College, Oxford
text: Type: organization

title: Keble College, Oxford
text:
Description
A college at Oxford opened in Keble’s memory, with the idea of widening and cheapening access to Oxford University, and bringing up a new generation of High Church clergymen. CMY attended the opening ceremony of its new buildings in 1876. For the college’s early and subsequent history see http://www.keble.ox.ac.uk/about/past/

title: Winchester Girls’ High School
text: Type: organization

title: Winchester Girls’ High School
text:
Description
This was a secondary school for girls founded in Winchester in 1884. The prime movers in its foundation were Anna Rachel Bramston and her friend Amélie Claire Leroy. CMY was a member of the school’s Council of Management from the foundation.The school changed its name to St. Swithun’s School in about 1930 and moved to the present site just outside the city in 1931. See http://www.stswithuns.com/school_history.htm

title: Winchester Girls’ High School
text:
Letters Mentioning This

Letter 3411

title: Atalanta
text: Type: periodical

title: Atalanta
text:
Description
This was a magazine for girls (1887-1896), edited until 1893 by L. T. Meade, which CMY considered populist and trivial in comparison with the Monthly Packet. She contributed to it ‘John Keble’ Atalanta (May 1888) 466-71. A conciliatory letter from Meade to CMY (MS Mrs Clare Roels, 4 September 1889) is evidently a reply to one in which CMY had complained of the ‘want of definite religion’ in the magazine.

title: Cassell’s Magazine
text: Type: periodical

title: Cassell’s Magazine
text:
Description
This magazine ran 1852-1934; Cassell’s also published several other magazines. The editor 1874-94 was H. G. B. Hunt. Arthur Butler worked for Cassell’s c. 1890-4.

title: Charm, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Charm, The
text:
Description
The Charm: A book for boys and girls Edited by J. C. was published by Addey and ran 1853-55. It was described by CMY in a letter to Macmillan as ‘a Child’s Magazine . . . which was so contrived that each subject occupied a sheet in each monthly part, and so when bound up all the history could be put together - all the science - all the story. But such a procrustean bed must have been misery to editor and contributors and I fancy was much aided by unlimited woodcut power.’

title: Christian Remembrancer, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Christian Remembrancer, The
text:
Description
This periodical had been published as a monthly magazine until 1844 when it was relaunched as a quarterly, at the height of the controversy over Newman’s conversion, as the organ of moderate Tractarian thought. Its editor from 1841 the Rev. William Scott (1813–1872), perpetual curate of Christ Church, Hoxton, and known as Scott of Hoxton; between 1844-55 he was joint editor with the Rev. James Bowling Mozley. It ran until 1868. In 1853, John Duke Coleridge published ‘Miss Yonge’s Novels’, a largely favourable assessment which all the same led to his incurring the wrath of FMY and made his next visit to Otterbourne uncomfortable. CMY herself wrote several long articles for it in the 1860s, which she several times mentions in connection with attempts to have them published in a volume: on Elizabeth Sewell’s Principles of Education; on ghosts and folklore; on the Sanitary Commission in the American war; on Lord Seaton and his regiment; on Eugenie de Guérin; on Alexandrine de la Ferronays; and on the Life of Bishop Mackenzie.

title: Church Quarterly Review, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Church Quarterly Review, The
text:
Description
This ran 1875-1968. It was edited from its foundation until his death by the Rev. A. R. Ashwell (1824–1879). CMY contributed a review of Edith Jacob’s ‘Thoughts on Invalid Life’.

title: Churchman’s Companion
text: Type: periodical

title: Churchman’s Companion
text:
Description
This periodical, which ran 1847-1883, was published by Joseph Masters, and considered in the Yonges’ circle rather too controversial in its treatment of religious topics to be suitable for young people. It thus inspired the foundation of the Monthly Packet. ‘Its tone was . . . extremely controversial, and it was given to insist more on the surface pecularities of the Church movement than the wiser members of that movement thought good. The Dysons, and possibly others, suggested the putting forth of a magazine for young people, suited to the schoolroom rather than the village school, and which should avoid personal controversy as unsuited to the young.’ (Coleridge, Life 164). CMY’s story Henrietta’s Wish was serialized in it (January 1849-May 1850) and also her The Two Guardians (July 1850-February 1852). The editor until 1858 was the Rev. William Balmborough Flower. He was followed by Masters' partner until 1863, when Felicia Skene took over, editing it to 1880.

title: Churchwoman, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Churchwoman, The
text:
Description
Gertrude Ireland Blackburne was co-editor of The Churchwoman (1897-1905). It may be the same as The English Churchwoman, which continued Our Paper : The monthly journal of the Church of England Women’s Help Society and appears to have existed June 1883-December 1911.

title: St. Mark’s College, Chelsea
text: Type: organization

title: St. Mark’s College, Chelsea
text:
Description
This was a training college for elementary schoolteachers, founded in 1841. Its first head was the Rev. Derwent Coleridge, son of the poet and father of Christabel Coleridge, who worked there until 1864. The music teaching there was provided by his friend Thomas Helmore, and transformed English church services. It is mentioned by the Rev. Derwent Coleridge (15 November 1873).

title: English Illustrated Magazine
text: Type: periodical

title: English Illustrated Magazine
text:
Description
This was published monthly by Macmillan 1883-1913. CMY published The Armourer’s Prentices in it in 1884. She wrote to G. L. Craik (10 July 1884) protesting about the publication in it of Henry James’s ‘The Author of Beltraffio’on the grounds that it was calculated to ‘make sceptically inclined people averse from devout faith’.

title: Newnham College
text: Type: organization

title: Newnham College
text:
Description
Founded as a hall for women students in 1871, and now a college of Cambridge University. There is a history by Gillian Sutherland on its website at http://www.newn.cam.ac.uk/about-newnham/College-History/History/content/History-of-the-college

title: Friendly Leaves
text: Type: periodical

title: Friendly Leaves
text:
Description
This was a periodical published by the Girls’ Friendly Society, and aimed at the working-class girls who were its members. It ran 1876-1951, renaming itself first The G. F. S. Magazine and then The Townsend.
Its first editor was Mary Elizabeth Townsend, the GFS's founder, and subsequently byAnne Mercier. In 1885 Eliza Keary became editor, and was succeeded in 1890 by Henley I. Arden, who was succeeded in 1899 by Christabel Rose Coleridge. It was published quarterly until 1877 when it became a monthly magazine.
The charity also published The Girls' Friendly Society Associates' Journal aimed at the upper-class ladies known as ‘associates’ as opposed to ‘members’, and a quarterly magazine, at first entitled Friendly Work and then the Girls' Quarterly, which was aimed at 'members' who were rather better educated than those reading Friendly Leaves.

title: Good Words
text: Type: periodical

title: Good Words
text:
Description
Described by CMY as ‘so Scottish and Presbyterian’. She wrote to Christabel Coleridge (11 August 1871) complaining that ‘respectable people’ could not be expected to read a love story by Matilda Betham-Edwards. She advised Christabel (1 Jan 1872) to avoid writing for it. Good Words was published from Jan 1860 to Apr 1911. According to Mrs Gatty it paid much better than the MP: 'Why does an orthodox parson’s wife write for Good Words? When orthodox magazines value her services at £1 per page and copyright reserved–instead of 2s.6d. (Monthly Packet !) –then you may wonder–but not till then.' (Maxwell, Mrs.Gatty and Mrs.Ewing, 132)

title: Guardian, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Guardian, The
text:
Description
This was a High Church weekly newspaper which ran 1846-1951, and is not to be confused with the daily newspaper formerly known as the Manchester Guardian which changed its name to The Guardian in 1959. The High Church Guardian had been founded by a group of Tractarians including CMY’s friend Lord Blachford. She herself seems to have regularly read it and also reviewed for it, but the precise extent of her involvement with it remains to be charted.

title: Guardian, The
text:

Letter 3394

title: Little Folks
text: Type: periodical

title: Little Folks
text:
Description
This was a children’s magazine (1871-1933).

title: Magazine for the Young
text: Type: periodical

title: Magazine for the Young
text:
Description
This periodical was founded by Mary Anne Dyson and her sister-in-law and published by James Burns, but edited from soon after the start by Anne Mozley (1809–1891), whose publisher brothers took over its publication in 1850; it ran 1842-1875. It was known in CMY’s circle as ‘the Pink Mag’; it was a tiny duodecimo production with a pink paper cover. CMY published in this periodical ‘Chapters on Flowers’ (February 1850-December 1852), which later appeared as The Herb of the Field (1853); twenty-seven ‘Langley School’ stories (September 1846-December 1848), fifteen of which appeared as Langley School; ‘Mrs Elderney’s School’ which was never reprinted in volume form (January 1850-Jan 1852); ‘Thoughts on Pictures’ (1853), also never republished; Friarswood Post Office (August 1858-August 1859); Countess Kate (October 1861-December 1862); and New Ground (January 1863-November 1866).

title: Magazine for the Young
text:
Letters Mentioning This

Letter 3386

title: Mission Life, or, The Emigrant and the Heathen
text: Type: periodical

title: Mission Life, or, The Emigrant and the Heathen
text:
Description
This was a periodical published by Rivington, which ran from 1866 to 1890. It was edited by the Rev. [[person:604]J. J. Halcombe], editorial secretary to the SPCK. It is not to be confused with the Mission Field, the organ of the SPG. CMY commented (1 Jan 1872) on the details of Bishop Patteson's death provided by Charles Hyde Brooke’s account in Mission Life.

title: Mothers in Council
text: Type: periodical

title: Mothers in Council
text:
Description
This quarterly was edited by CMY 1890-1901. It ran until 1930. It was aimed at the educated members of the Mothers’ Union, as distinct from The Mothers’ Union Journal, which was aimed at the working-class mothers.

title: Once a Week

Mentioned (9 April 1860).

title: Sharpe’s Magazine
text: Type: periodical

title: Sharpe’s Magazine
text:
Description
Sharpe’s London Magazine of Entertainment and Instruction ran 1846-1870. Menella Bute Smedley and her cousin Frank Smedley both wrote for it, and the latter was briefly its editor.

title: St. Nicholas
text: Type: periodical

title: St. Nicholas
text:
Description
An American magazine for children, it ran 1873-1941. CMY wanted The Release to be published in it, but this does not seem to have happened. Louisa May Alcott, ‘Pansies’ St. Nicholas 15 (November 1887) has a girl say: ‘” I love dear Miss Yonge and her books, with their nice, large families, and their trials, and their pious ways, and pleasant homes full of brothers and sisters, and good fathers and mothers. I’m never tired of them, and have read ’Daisy Chain’ nine times at least.”’

title: Orthopaedic Hospital
text: Type: organization

title: Orthopaedic Hospital
text:
Description
The Royal National Orthopaedic Hospital, originally called the Infirmary for the Cure of Club Foot and other Contractions, and later the Orthopaedic Institution, was founded in 1838 and opened at Bloomsbury Square in 1840.  Its archives are in London Metropolitan Archives. See http://www.rnoh.nhs.uk/about-the-rnoh/history-the-rnoh.

title: Month, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Month, The
text:
Description
This Catholic journal ran 1864-2001. Between 1865 and 1881 it was edited by CMY’s cousin Father Henry James Coleridge S. J., and became associated with the Society of Jesus. She mentions it (27 April 1867).

title: Mothers’ Union Journal, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Mothers’ Union Journal, The
text:
Description
This periodical, which ran under this name 1888-1954, was aimed at the working-class members of the Mothers’ Union, as distinct from the educated readers of Mothers in Council. It changed its name to Home and Family in 1954 and in 2008 to Families First and currently (2010) is also published online:
http://www.themothersunion.org/familiesfirst.aspx
The first editor was Madalene Sabine (Pasley) Jenkyns (1847/8-1935).

title: Nonconformist, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Nonconformist, The
text:
Description
This was a newspaper founded in 1841 by a dissenting clergyman, the Rev. Edward Miall, who edited it until 1877. It advocated non-payment of tithes and the disestablishment of the Church of England; CMY was as unlikely to sympathise with its views as it was with hers. See D. W. Bebbington, ‘Miall, Edward (1809–1881)’, Oxford Dictionary of National Biography, Oxford University Press, Sept 2004.

title: Reader: A review of current literature, science, and art, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Reader: A review of current literature, science, and art, The
text:
Description
Mentioned 11 August 1863. It ran January 1863 - July 1866.

title: Saturday Review, The
text: Type: periodical

title: Saturday Review, The
text:
Description
This was a weekly magazine of conservative tendency, notorious for its trenchant reviews. It was immediately successful on its foundation in 1855; its first editor was John Douglas Cook (1808?-1868).

title: The Rev. J. F. W. Bullock - Daily Lections for Every Morning and Evening throughout the Year
text:
Date: 1902
Imprint: London: Rivington
Notes: In 1902 Bullock published the first three volumes of a projected six volume work called Daily Lections for every Morning and Evening throughout the Year (London: Rivington 1902). Volume V of this work, described in his preface, was to have consisted of 'Lections on Anglican Worthies, arranged as additional Readings for Sunday'. CMY contributed several of these 'Biographies of Eminent Anglicans', which exist in MS and are catalogued under that name among the Bullock papers in the Bodleian Library, Oxford, but were never published, since no more of the Daily Lections ever appeared.

title: Ann Maria Carter Smith - ‘The Lost are Found’
text:
Date: 1860
Imprint: MP (May 1860) 496-517
Notes: In this story a young navel cadet joins the ill-fated Franklin expedition to the Arctic, taking a slipper worked by his sister as a memento of home. Fourteen yers later the position of the slipper among the relics found by M’Clintock tell his mother and sister that he died before his suffering could be prolonged. The story was reprinted in Lucy and Christian Wainwright and Other Stories.

Date: 1861

Notes: Jane Hooper had a daughter Margaret L. Hooper (b. Middlesex 1854/5), who became an artist and illustrator and a son, Winnard Hooper (1853-1935), city editor of The Times. CMY's letter to Hooper perhaps implies that they were the characters in the story. CMY recommended it in ’Hints on Reading' MP (March 1862) 335, despite having told Ann Carter Smith she didn't care for it.

title: Francis Brandt - ‘The cyclone at Masulipatam’
text:
Date: 1865
Imprint: MP (March 1865) 319-327
Notes: The article is signed 'F. B.'. It was evidently sent to CMY by one of the sisters of Francis Brandt (1839/40-1925), the assistant collector on whose letters it is based. His three sisters were Margaret Sophia Brandt (1831/2-1892), Emily Charlotte Brandt (1834/5-1926) and Margaret [sic] Adelaide Brandt (1845/6-1904). No doubt the additional text was composed by one of them, but it is not possible to say which.

title: Mary (Kingsman) Bargus
text:
(1759?-1843)
maternal grandmother of CMY
Coleridge states, [[otherbook:40]Life], 154 that CMY's grandmother died in 1848. However, it seems more probable that she died in 1843, when the death was registered in Winchester of a person of that name. She was daughter of the Rev. Thomas Kingsman (1717-1779), Vicar of Botley, and married (1792), as his second wife, the Rev. Thomas Bargus. 'I think she was 89' when she died, wrote CMY. The IGI records the christening of a Mary Kingsman at Botley in 1751, which would make her about 92 in 1843, and 44 at the time of FMY's birth in 1795. Her father's death is recorded in the Town and Country Magazine (1780), 55.

title: Mary (Kingsman) Moore
text:
(b.c.1785)
cousin of FMY
The Mr and Mrs Moore mentioned in these early letters are perhaps the same as Mrs More who gave warm shawls to Otterbourne schoolchildren in December 1844;  Mrs Moore sent a parcel 24 Sept 1846 including Mr Kingsman's chain. All these references have therefore been rather boldly associated with Mary Kingsman, a niece of FMY's mother Mary (Kingsman) Bargus, daughter of her brother William George Kingsman (b.1755). The younger Mary Kingsman married (Otterbourne 1823) Stephen Roger Moore. In 1848 she proved the will of her uncle John Kingsman (1764-1848) who left legacies to her, FMY, JBY, CMY and the Rev. Thomas Westcombe. She can probably be identified with the Maria Kingsman mentioned in the CMY's autobiographical chapters (Coleridge, Life, 7), the orphan niece who was a member of the Bargus household during FMY's childhood. Details of the descendants of CMY's great-grandfather the Rev. Thomas Kingsman (1717-1779), Vicar of Botley, are at http://freepages.genealogy.rootsweb.ancestry.com/~kingsman/kingsman/england/indiI13063.html

title: Cordelia Anne Duke (Yonge) Oldfield
text:
(1807-1864)
cousin of CMY
Daughter of WCY’s eldest brother, the Rev. Duke Yonge, Vicar of Antony, and Cordelia (Colborne) Yonge, stepsister of FMY. This younger Cordelia Yonge, known as Delia, married (1849), as his third wife, General John Oldfield (1789-2 Aug 1863), aide de camp to the Queen Her sole executor was her brother Dr John Francis Duke Yonge.  Her husband had 15 children by his first and second wives and none by her.  His executor was his son Colonel John Rawdon Oldfield of Oldfield Lawn, Westbourne  [information from Ian Yonge]. Delia Oldfield's dates were 25 Nov 1807-6 Sept 1864.

title: Catherine Barter
text:
(b.1818/9)
traveller and schoolmistress
The daughter of the Rev. Charles Barter, Rector of Sarsden, and niece of the Rev. Robert Speckott Barter, she seems to have lived near her brother the Rev. Henry Barter, Vicar of Shipton-under-Wychwood, and run (in 1871) a small school, whose pupils included a South African ward named Salome Melango. CMY calls her ‘Kate Barter’ and refers to her ‘young Zulus’. She is not to be confused with her sister-in-law Elspeth Catherine ‘Kitty’ (Moberly) Barter, or with her first cousin Katherine Mary (Barter) Wordsworth. She was the author of an anonymous travel book Alone among the Zulus: The narrative of a journey through the Zulu country, South Africa. By a plain woman (London: SPCK 1866) and has an entry in S. O. Spencer, British settlers in Natal, 1824-1857: A Biographical Register (2001). She was born at Broadstone, Oxfordshire.

title: Harriet Poole
text:
(b. Mansfield, Nottingham 1843/4)
governess
She was governess at the time of the 1871 census to the children of the Rev. Robert Hughes at Wyelands House, Matherne, Wales; by 1881 she was living in London with her sister [[person:1188]Alice], both governesses. In the 1901 census she is a Sister of Charity at the sisterhood of St. Thomas, Oxford. CMY implies that Mary Anne Dyson knew her, which increases the probability that she was the Elgies’ governess, who had previously worked in Miss Dyson’s school, and read German with Gertrude. Probably the Miss Poole who offered 'Rosa's Dream' in 1867.

title: Charles Warren Adams
text:
(1833-30 July 1903)
animal rights campaigner
He married, first (1861), Georgina Alethe Polson (b.1838), daughter of the Rev. Hugh Polson and Georgiana, only child of Charlotte Yonge and her first husband Captain George Crawley, and therefore CMY’s first cousin once removed.  (Mrs Crawley was CMY’s aunt and godmother and married secondly the Rev Thomas Jones, Rector of Exeter College Oxford). The Polsons had other children: (Arthur b. 1831/2, George b. 1833/4, Rosalia b.1834/5 and Catharina K. b. 1835/6). Adams married secondly (24 June 1885), in the teeth of her family, the Hon. Mildred Mary Coleridge (1847-14 January 1929). For his involvement in brawling during a church service at St. George in the East, in defence of the innovations introduced by the vicar, see The Times (11, 14 April 1860). [LG 1952 Woollcombe Adams of Anstey Hall; 1841, 1851, 1861 censuses at www.ancestry.co.uk]

title: Mary R. Pridham
text:
(b. 1849/50)
member of the Gosling Society, contributor to MP (1891)
A Gosling as ‘Hedgerose’ from c. 1865, living then at 6 Trevor Terrace Rutland Gate, she was born Mauritius, daughter of John Pridham, schoolmaster; she was first cousin of Christabel Coleridge and Katherine Helmore.  Her mother remarried William Martyn MRCS (1815-96);  in 1881 she was living 63 Harcourt Terrace in the household of Thomas Leon Wilkinson, perhaps as a governess, in 1891 with her stepfather and mother at Westmoreland Lodge, Inner Park Rd. Wandsworth [1871, 1881 census; Plarr, Lives of FRCS; Hainton, The Unknown Coleridge; the Plymouth Herald (7 April 1838) records the death of John Drake Pridham (1777/8-1838), of the firm of Harris Rosdew and Co, bankers of this town;  he  was father of John Pridham, Mrs Helmore and Mrs Derwent Coleridge]

title: Elizabeth P. Roberts
text:
contributor to MP (1852-9)
The author of the ‘Garland for the Year’, who preserved the letters which shed such a useful light on the early history of MP, signed her contributions E. P. R. or Elizabeth P. Roberts. She may perhaps be identified with Elizabeth Piddocke Roberts (b. Kingswinford, Staffs. 1821) who published a book of verse in 1845. That person was the daughter of John Piddocke Roberts (b.1794/5) and his wife Susannah Maria Morris (b. 1789/90), who had been married at Kingswinford in 1819; he is described in the 1841 census of Armitage, Staffs. as a land agent, and in the 1851 Isle of Man census as a landed proprietor. By 1861 Elizabeth and her widowed mother were staying in West Bromwich, and she is described as a governess. Since the 'Garland' author's father evidently died in 1859 it is likely they are one and the same.
According to the IGI records she was one of at least ten children, of whom several died young. Survivors included Rebecca Surgy Roberts (b. 1831) who acted as a companion to a cousin in the Isle of Wight (1851, 1861 census), Phoebe Cordelia Roberts (1828-after 1881) and Martha Sarah Roberts (1835-1909) who married (1852) Thomas Oxenbould, a banker’s clerk.
Roberts’s ‘Garland for the Year’ may possibly be the ‘semi botanical papers’ of which CMY wrote ‘I used to repent - of admitting them’.
[1841 census, Armitage with Handsacre, Staffs.; parents Isle of Man 1851 census; 1861 census with cousin Lydia Morris, chemist, at West Bromwich; Oxenboulds, 1871, 1891 census, Handsworth; BMD indexes; IGI]

title: Beatrice Braithwaite (Stebbing) Batty
text:
(1833-1893)
contributor to MP
Daughter of the Rev. Henry Stebbing, her marriage (24 July 1860) to the Rev. Robert Braithwaite Batty, elder son of the late Lieut-Col Robert Batty, Grenadier Guards, was announced in The Times (26 July 1860). She appears to have been sister-in-law to Johanna Batty. According to her obituary The Times (2 May 1933) her husband went as a missionary to India and died almost immediately. Some autobiographical and other MSS of hers are in the Bodleian Library, Oxford.
