title: Harriet Catherine (Carr) Romanoff
text:
(1827/8-1897)
contributor to MP (1861-77)
CMY’s Russian correspondent, she came from a family who were friends and neighbours of Florence Wilford in Woolwich; her mother Harriet Catherine Carr also contributed to MP. Monuments in St. George’s Beckenham include: James Joshua CARR of Old Charlton Kent 22 May 1865 aged 67. Harriet Catherine Carr widow of above 27 August 1895 aged 90. Samuel Sidney Carr 13 August 1842 aged 1 year 9 months. Captain James Sweet Carr of H M Bombay Staff Corps 28 February 1864 aged 31 sons of James Joshua and Harriet Catherine Carr. Also Janet Susan Carr youngest daughter of J J and H C Carr 16 July 1919 aged 75. Also Harriet Catherine ROMANOFF daughter of J J and H C Carr 10 August 1897 aged 69 at Kishinieff Russia. [This is now Kishinev, capital of Moldova, and was scene of a notorious pogrom in 1903]

title: Captain Raphael Semmes
text:
(1809-1877)
captain of the Alabama
A confederate warship was built anonymously in the British shipyards at Birkenhead. When the Unionist government discovered this they demanded that it be detained in the yard, but in 1862, on the pretext of a trial run, Semmes and his crew escaped with it to the Azores where it was armed and proceeded to do immense damage to Unionist shipping, to the commercial benefit of the British. In 1864 the Alabama was sunk off Cherbourg but Semmes was picked up by an English yacht and brought to England. CMY referred to the Alabama affair disapprovingly in the Barnacle.

title: Mary Emily Simpson
text:
(1820-1884)
educational reformer
The author of Ploughing and Sowing was the daughter of the Rev. Francis Simpson, Vicar of Boynton, and of Anne Strickland (d.1868), daughter of Sir William Strickland, 6th Bt. She was not exactly niece of Sir Henry Boynton 10th Bt. (1811-69) of Burton Agnes, as CMY says, but his first wife Louisa Strickland (d.1841) was her mother’s first cousin, and other marriages connected the two families. There is a study: Clifford Ball Freeman, Mary Simpson of Boynton Vicarage, teacher of ploughboys and critic of Methodism, East Yorkshire Local History Society Series 28 (1972)

title: Walter Smith
text:
publisher
He ‘took the Mozleys’ business when John Mozley died . . . trained at Macmillans . . . a gentleman and pleasant to deal with in all ways’ (1882). He was associated with CMY 1874-1887. He moved the office from 6 Paternoster Row to 34 King Street Covent Garden, in 1881. The note (MS Ewelme Cottage Museum) accompanying payment for Annie Lush's article 'A Trip to Norfolk Island' has the printed address in Paternoster Row crossed out and the new address written in; it is dated 24 May 1881.

title: Anne Sturges Bourne
text:
(1809-9 Dec 1891)
landowner
Daughter of William Sturges Bourne (1769-1845), she was a rich philanthropic spinster. A lifelong friend of [[person:464]Mary Anne Dyson], they shared interests in educational and charitable work. Their very copious correspondence, with much detail on Tractarian projects and much gossip, survives in Hampshire Record Office. Together they encouraged the young CMY’s philanthropic and literary work. Anne Sturges Bourne appears to have been the author of the review of My Life and What Shall I Do With It? which appeared in 1861. [Her death aged 92 was reported in the Daily News 15 Dec 1891]

title: Lady Helena (Perceval) Trench
text:
(1790/1-17 March 1881)
mother of Maria (Trench) Wilson
She was a sister of the 6th Earl of Egmont and of Sir William Heathcote’s first wife; she married (9 Jan 1816) the Rev. Frederick Stuart Trench (19 March 1787-23 Nov 1860).  She lived with her daughter Mrs Wilson at Rownhams by 1867. She was aunt by marriage to Frances Dorothy (Grimston) Moor; another of her daughters Helena Trench (1820-1908) married the Very Rev. Jeffrey Lefroy, Dean of Dromore, and was the mother of George Alfred Lefroy, Bishop of Calcutta (1854–1919) [PB 1914 Ashtown;  PO Hants 1867; Erminois; ODNB]

title: Gertrude Walter
text:
(Shotapoor 24 May 1849-20 May 1897)
housemate of CMY, sister-in-law of JBY
Daughter of Edward Walter and Mary Emma Coulthard. Her sister Frances married (1858) CMY's only brother Julian Bargus Yonge. She moved into Elderfield with CMY in about 1872.  Her illness began 8 years before 1871.
[Bombay Baptisms N/3/24/118; 'she would have been 48 on the 24th']
CMY described her movingly in ‘Our Tender Ones’ Mothers in Council 9 (January 1899) 20-27, 26:
‘Let me draw the picture of a real invalid not long passed away. She was a bright active girl of fourteen when rheumatic fever set in, followed by arthritis. Gradually, during nearly twenty years, every power was taken from her; first one knee was affected, then the other, then she could no longer sit at the piano, she moved on crutches, and drove in a donkey chaise; but in time, always after severe illness and pain, these pleasures failed her, she could only be carried from one room to another or to church on a couch. She lost her voice, and next the use of her right arm, and for several years was entirely confined to bed.’

title: Edward Charles Lethbridge Walter

(Bishopstoke Hants. 1854-5 May 1885 Alderney)
half-brother of Gertrude Walter and of Frances (Walter) Yonge
He was gazetted to the 83rd Foot Regiment in 1875. He married Mabel Blanche Waller in July 1884. His death from an accident was reported in the Ipswich Journal 14 May 1885. He left a baby son, Edward Walter.

title: The Rev.Canon William Parsons Warburton
text:
(1824/5-1913)
school inspector
He was educated at Balliol College, Oxford, and was a Fellow of All Souls 1849-53. He served as HM Inspector of Schools 1851-81, and of training schools 1881-5. He was an honorary Canon of Winchester 1878-81, and was made a Canon in 1885. He had three daughters Mary A. (b.1859/60), Katharine (b. 1861/2), and Charlotte (b. 1864/5) [Rugby School Register, Alumni Oxonienses]

title: Miss Warren
text:
The Miss Warren and Gundred referred to by CMY in 1900 were possibly Louisa Warren and her sister Gundred (b. Shropshire 1822/3) who appear in the 1891 census living at Market Drayton, but not in any earlier one, though a Georgiana Gundred Warren (birth registered Market Drayton 1839) appears with oddly varying dates of birth in the census returns.
Unusual as Gundred is as a name, it was that of the wife (d.1085), of William de Warenne (d.1088), and therefore seems to have been chosen by people named Warren unexpectedly often.

Educated at Westminster and Trinity College, Cambridge, priest 1829, Headmaster of Westminster School 1828-1846, Rector of Sutton Coldfield 1844-50, Vicar of Pershore 1850-66, Hon. Canon of Worcester; he married (1833) Anne Gray, daughter of the Bishop of Bristol, uncle of Ann Carter Smith. title: Caroline Birley
text:
(1851-1907)
author of children's fiction 1880-94, contributor to MP (1883-6)
Her works are often commended by CMY in 'Hints on Reading'. She was a keen amateur geologist who bequeathed her collections to the British Museum. [obituary The Times (28 February 1907) 10; others in science journals]
She is therefore unlikely to be the Miss Birley lending CMY Salverte's book on names in 1864, when she would have been rather young.

title: Jemima (Wedderburn) Blackburn
text:
(1823-1909)
artist and naturalist
Jemima (Wedderburn) Blackburn (1823-1909), a Scottish naturalist and painter, met CMY through [[person:1252]Frederic Rogers] (1811-1889), later Lord Blachford, a Devon Tractarian who was married to her cousin Georgina Colvile (d.1900). Blackburn’s memories of CMY are printed in Robert Fairley, [[otherbook:63]Jemima: Paintings and Memoirs of a Victorian Lady] (Edinburgh : Canongate, 1988), 128: ‘Charlotte Yonge was a friend of the Rogers and was asked to stay while I was visiting them. It was a very small house and we were lodged in two little garrets next one another, and used to brush out our hair together before going to bed, and so soon became friends. She was very good looking with dark hair and fine eyes, spoke pleasantly and was a little above middle height. We talked of many things. I found her not at all formidable, as one feared she might be, being so well informed; one might almost say learned. . . . I kept up the friendship as long as she lived, and got a good many letters from her while I was illustrating her books.’ She married Hugh Blackburn, Professor of Mathematics at Edinburgh University.
Jemima Blackburn discovered the habits of the cuckoo.

title: The Rev. Duke Yonge
text:
(21 June 1823-7 Oct 1881)
cousin of CMY
The fourth and only surviving of the five sons of the Rev. John Yonge of Puslinch, and Alethea Henrietta (Bargus) Yonge, he inherited Puslinch in 1877 (John, James, John Bargus and Edmund Charles having predeceased him). He was curate of Newton Ferrars in 1849, and subsequently Rector of Newton Ferrars like his father. He married (26 Aug 1862) Charlotte Cordelia Pode (1839/40-7 Sept 1929), who was his second cousin (she was CMY’s first cousin).  He shared two of her eight great-grandparents, whereas he shared four of CMY’s eight great-grandparents. According to a letter of his youngest daughter Alethea’s, he had wished to marry CMY.

title: Mary Elizabeth Mills Bromfield
text:
1824/5-1888
contributor to MP
The identity of this person is slightly puzzling, though CMY called her one of 'my crack authors'.
The most likely candidate seems to be Mary Elizabeth (Mills) Bromfield (Harrow 1824/5-1888 Newton Abbot), daughter of the Rev. Dr William Mills, master of Exeter Grammar School, in 1861 living with an engineer brother in Canterbury. She married (Canterbury 23 Sept 1862) the Rev. Henry Bromfield, Vicar of Blockley, and was living with him there 1871. In 1881 she was a widow with an aged spinster sister-in-law at Fladbury House, Pershore, the address with which she corresponds with MP. She was probably the Mary E. Bromfield, author of SPCK tales, Female Christian Names &c., in COPAC.
Another possibility is that she is also the contributor to Biographies of Good Women described as ‘the author of Magdalen Stafford’. That novel, Magdalen Stafford, or, A Gleam of Sunshine on a Rainy Day (1857) and the same author’s The Home Life of English Ladies in the XVII Century (1860), may be Mrs Bromfield’s work.

title: Lady Susanna (Nelson) Blunt
text:
(1829/30-8 April 1900)
She was daughter of the 2nd Earl Nelson, who changed his name from Bolton on succeeding his uncle in 1835, and sister of the 3rd Earl Nelson. She was therefore related to Alexander Montgomery Moore. At a fairly advanced age she married (27 June 1865) the Rev. Canon Alexander Colvin Blunt. She was the president of the GFS in Winchester diocese in 1878. Her mother and CMY’s had been friends.  [PB Blunt Bt. Nelson E]

title: Julia Lucy Woodward
text:
Cheltenham 1850-1910
probably a member of the Spider Society as 'Bath Brick'
Daughter of Richard Woodward (d.1875), retired Bengal Civil Service, she lived in Bath in 1871 and 1881
and 1891 with her widowed mother at the Knoll, Clevedon. She was a fellow
of the Royal Meterological Society, a member of the Ex Libris Society
(collecting bookplates), and a supporter of local history societies and
publications. She contributed a memoir of her uncle Vincent Stuckey Lean to
his book on proverbs (1902-4). She compiled the autograph album in which the
letters to Bath Brick were found; since Bath Brick is known to have lived
near Clevedon they were probably the same person.

title: Mary (Bargus) Bogue
text:
1746-1806
great-aunt of CMY
The Gentleman’s Magazine (1806) 760 records the death of the widow of Dr. Bogue, of Fareham, eldest daughter of Richard Bargus of Fareham. If she was the Mary Bargus christened at Fareham in 1746 she was the child of Richard Bargus’s first wife Frances Eyer, and thus probably a full sister of CMY's maternal grandfather the Rev. Thomas Bargus. She was probably married at Fareham in 1775, though the IGI transcribes her husband’s name as Bogrie. The Rev. John Richard Bogue, whom CMY describes as her cousin, was probably her grandson.

great-uncle of CMY

CMY in Coleridge, Life, 6 states that he died in the same epidemic as Lord Brooke (1772-1786), son of the 2nd Earl of Warwick and his first wife Georgiana Peachey (whose father Lord Selsey was Thomas Bargus’s patron). John Lock Bargus appears in Alumni Oxonienses and Register of Gray’s Inn.

title: Elizabeth Anderson Morshead
text:
cousin of CMY
Elizabeth Yonge, sister of CMY's grandfather the Rev. Duke Yonge, married a local landowner, Philip Morshead of Widey Court, Egg Buckland, Plymouth. They had an only daughter Elizabeth Morshead, who married Colonel Anderson and had 11 children called Anderson Morshead. Of these the heir, the Rev. John Philip Anderson Morshead married (1845)his second cousin Alethea Yonge of Puslinch, Pentire was the father of Mary Anderson Morshead. Their sister Elizabeth Morshead, referred to by Anne Yonge, died in Devon in 1849. [Burke LG 1871; death register]

title: Elizabeth Anderson Morshead

They had an only daughter Elizabeth Morshead, who married Colonel Anderson and had 11 children called Anderson Morshead. Of these the heir, the Rev. John Philip Anderson Morshead married (1845)his second cousin Alethea Yonge of Puslinch, Pentire was the father of Mary Anderson Morshead. Their sister Elizabeth Morshead, referred to by Anne Yonge, died in Devon in 1849. [Burke LG 1871; death register]

title: Elizabeth Birt Alice (Holmes, then Reed) Yonge
text:
(1799/1800-1863)
cousin-in-law of CMY
Elizabeth (Holmes) Reed married, as her third husband, CMY's cousin Dr. John Francis Duke Yonge, son of the Rev. Duke Yonge of Antony, who was about 14 years her junior: they had three sons: Francis Arthur Holmes Yonge, James Frederick Moore Yonge, and Stephen Duke Yonge. He became a Roman Catholic in the late 1840s, to the consternation of his family, but according to CMY, she did not. [ Her marriage, under the name Elizabeth Bird Alice Reed, is recorded in Brussels, 1839 by the IGI; advertisement for administration of estate of J.V.C. Reed in the Morning Post 16 April 1864, 1]
She was the daughter of Richard Holmes, of Westcott, St. Dominick, Cornwall, and Elizabeth Nash.
She was first married (18 November 1815), aged not quite 16, in the church at Antony, Cornwall, to Cavalie Shorthouse Mercer (1789-1819), with whom she had two children, Theodosia Alice Sterling Mercer (1817-1832) and Henrietta Fyers (Mercer) Muller (1818-1839). He died in Bermuda, leaving her a widow of 19. She remarried (1822) Captain John Reed (d. 1835) with whom she had at least six children, including Eliza Holmes Reed, who married Thomas C. Watson, and John Vaughan Carden Reed (d. 1858) Lieutenant in the Royal Marine Light Infantry. She had perhaps known the much younger John Francis Duke Yonge since his childhood, since he had been brought up in Antony.
Information from Mr Graeme Hicks.

title: The Rt. Rev. Benjamin Treadwell Onderdonk
text:
(1791-1861)
Bishop of New York
He was consecrated bishop in 1831 but accusations of sexual misconduct resulted in his suspension in the 1840s. Disputes raged over the justice of this between the tractarian and evangelical wings of the American episcopal church until his death. It was not he, but the Bishop of western New York, the Rt. Rev. William Heathcote DeLancey, whom CMY met at the SPG meeting in Winchester in 1852.

Margaret's mother is described as a former lady's maid. Ellen Brown (b. Cranbrook, Kent 1825/6) was lady's maid to Sturges Bourne at the time of the 1851 census. She married Laidlaw in the New Forest in 1852, and had several small children at the time of her death in the April quarter of 1860. [1851 census; death and marriage registers]

title: Owen Browne Carter
text:
1802/3-1859)
architect
Carter was employed to draw plans for the church of St. Matthew Otterbourne, though CMY always maintained that her father did most of the work. He was a Winchester architect, and worked on the restoration of the cathedral. In 1856 he was accused, but acquitted, of forgery. His obituary speaks darkly of eccentricities and a death in poverty. [Hampshire Advertiser (9 February 1856) 6; Salisbury Guardian (2 April 1859) 4.]

title: Mary (Fowler) Calvert
text:
(1814-1882)
missionary in Fiji
She was the wife of the Methodist missionary James Calvert (1813-1892), who worked in Mbau in the 1850s. With Mrs Richard Lyth she pleaded for the lives of 14 women who were to be killed and eaten. The incident is described in James Calvert, Fiji and the Fijians (London : Alexander Heylin, 1858, rpt. Suva: Fiji Museum 2003) Chapter 8, 290-1.

title: Sergeant Amos Humiston
text:
(1830-1863)
Union soldier killed at the battle of Gettysburg
Drew Gilpin Faust, This Republic of Suffering: Death and the American Civil War (New York: Vintage 2008), 11, describes the currency of this story: 'A dead Yankee soldier at Gettysburg was found with an ambrotype of three children \“tightly clasped in his hands.\” The ultimately successful effort to identify him created a sensation, with magazine and newspaper articles, poems, and songs celebrating the devoted father, who perished with his eyes and heart focused on eight-year-old Franklin, six-year-old Alice, and four-year-old Frederick.'

Henrietta Robertson was the subject of Anne Mackenzie's book [[otherbook:417]Mission Life among the Zulu-Kafirs: Memorials of Henrietta Robertson wife of the Rev. R. Robertson compiled chiefly from letters and journals written to the late Bishop Mackenzie and his sisters] (Cambridge: Deighton 1866) and she also figures in CMY’s [[cmybook:90]Pioneers and Founders, or, Recent Workers in the Mission Field] (London: Macmillan 1871).

title: The Very Rev. John Bramston
text:
(1802-1887)
Vicar of Great Baddow, Essex and Dean of Winchester
He married (1832) Clarissa Sandford Trant (1800-1844) and had three children: Clara (1833-1907), wife of the Rev Bixby Garnham Luard (b. 1835) by whom she had 5 sons and 6 daughters;  Mary Eliza (1841-1912); and John Trant (1843-1931).  He married secondly (1845) Anna Hanbury (b.1805/6) and had one daughter Anna Rachel.  He lived at the Deanery, in Winchester Cathedral Close.  He was nephew of Mary Archer Houblon of Hallingbury. [C.G.Luard, Journal of Clarissa Trant;  LG 1906 Luard]

title: Mrs. Curteis
text:
author of 'Mrs. Twitch'
'Mrs. Twitch' was a story, signed R. L. C., published in MP (June 1869) 606-12, which gave rise to a query answered in (October 1869) 415. Two later stories, also about Mrs Twitch and presumably by the same author, were 'Pin-Baskets' (June 1875) 639-646, and 'True and Just in All My Dealings' (September 1881) 300-3. 'St. Canace's Jackdaw' was a poem, published in MP (August 1869) 140-1. The other stories mentioned in CMY's letter to Mrs Curteis have not been traced.

The identity of Helen Holbeche of Sutton Coldfield with this Miss Holbeche is confirmed by God is love, or, Memorials of Little Nony(London: Nisbet 1883) 84.

title: Miss M. Watson
text:
contributor to MP (1891)
CMY refers to 'Miss Watson and her German', which suggests that she was the author of the series of articles 'Studies in German Literature' which appeared in MP (January-June 1891). These were accompanied by an essay competition, and contestants were asked to send answers to 'Miss Watson, Brightwell, Tetsworth, Oxon..
The 1891 census return shows a Margaret Watson (b. Oxfordshire 1858/9), sister of a land agent, living with him at Brightwell Baldwin, which is reasonably near Tetsworth, who seems a possible candidate.

title: The Rev. John Frederick Watkinson Bullock
text:
(1840-1918)
He succeeded his father and grandfather as Rector of Radwinter 1865-1916. In 1902 he published the first three volumes of a projected six volume work called Daily Lections for every Morning and Evening throughout the Year (London: Rivington 1902). Volume V of this work, described in his preface, was to have consisted of 'Lections on Anglican Worthies, arranged as additional Readings for Sunday'. These 'Biographies of Eminent Anglicans' exist in MS and are catalogued under that name among the Bullock papers in the Bodleian Library, Oxford, but were never published, since no more of the Daily Lections ever appeared. The biographies were contributed by various authors, including CMY, Elizabeth Rundle Charles and Florence Wilford. [ Burke LG 1862, Venn, Alumni Cantabrigienses. N.B. Venn's statement that there is an obituary in the Guardian 11 April 1918 seems to be inaccurate.]

title: The Rev. John Frederick Watkinson Bullock

Volume V of this work, described in his preface, was to have consisted of 'Lections on Anglican Worthies, arranged as additional Readings for Sunday'. These 'Biographies of Eminent Anglicans' exist in MS and are catalogued under that name among the Bullock papers in the Bodleian Library, Oxford, but were never published, since no more of the Daily Lections ever appeared. The biographies were contributed by various authors, including CMY, Elizabeth Rundle Charles and Florence Wilford. [ Burke LG 1862, Venn, Alumni Cantabrigienses. N.B. title: Arthur John Butler
text:
(21 June 1844-26 Feb 1910)
contributor to MP, Italian scholar, son of the Very Rev. William John Butler
He married (6 April 1875) Mary Caroline Humphry. He was a fellow of Trinity College, Cambridge until 1870, worked in education office until 1887, became a partner in Rivington then Cassell, in 1894 became assistant commissioner on secondary education.  In 1899 joined PRO as editor of foreign state papers.  Translator. In 1898 he also became professor of Italian literature at UCL. [ODNB]

title: Charles Duke Yonge
text:
(1812-1891)
historian, cousin of CMY
CMY never mentions this cousin, who was the eldest son of her [[person:1586]Eton uncle], and some of whose long list of publications have sometimes been confused with some of hers. The Standard (1 April 1837) records his appearance before the Insolvent Debtors Court in connection with a long list of debts, amounting to over £2000, which he was unable to pay. He was described as the son of a widow who allowed him £250 a year, who had taken a first-class degree at Oxford, but had aspired 'to the reputation of fashionable men.' This episode is not mentioned by the ODNB, which records his later career, but might have created prejudice against him in the family.

title: The Very Rev. William John Butler
text:
(10 February 1818-14 January 1894)
Vicar of Wantage and Dean of Lincoln
He was ordained in 1841 and served as curate of Dogmersfield under Mary Anne Dyson’s brother. He served (1846-1880) as Vicar of Wantage, where he embarked on Tractarian improvements on a very large scale, including the establishment of the sisterhood of St. Mary the Virgin. He married (1843) Emma Barnett, who was his second cousin. She and her sister Elizabeth Barnett were close friends of CMY. In 1880 he moved to become a canon of Worcester, and in 1885 Dean of Lincoln; he was made DD in 1886. [ODNB; Life and Letters of Dean Butler]

title: The Rev. Richard Carter Smith
text:
(1802-1864)
perpetual curate of Charlton-next-Woolwich, father of Ann Carter Smith
He was christened Morwenstow, Cornwall, 29 August 1802, son of William and Ann Smith; and married, at Campton, near Bedford, on 24 November 1825, Mary Williamson, daughter of the Rev. Edmund (Boase has Edmond) Williamson (c.1760-1839), Rector of Campton with Shefford, Beds., and his wife Phoebe Riland.  Smith was admitted, late in life, a fellow commoner at Caius College, Cambridge in 1844, proceeded BA and MA in 1849 and 1860, was ordained deacon in 1848 and priest in 1849.  He served as curate of Sutton Coldfield, Warwickshire 1848-50, Pershore 1851-3, Stepney 1854-7, and Charlton-next-Woolwich from 1862, being appointed to the perpetual curacy of St. Pauls Charlton.  He died aged 62 on 1 October 1864. It looks as if Richard Carter Smith was emulating one of his brothers-in-law in retraining for the church in the crisis years of the early 1840s, and that after he was ordained he acted as curate to another brother-in-law for five years. He has  BL or Copac entries but the Harvard library catalogue lists Richard Carter Smith, Reise i Norge 1838 overs av Johnny Johnsen (Oslo, Univertitesforl c.1976). This corroborates the evidence that the Carter Smith family had spent time in Norway. [IGI; Alumni Cantabrigienses, there is a brief death notice in the Gentleman’s Magazine.]

title: Anne Elizabeth Mary Anderson Morshead
text:
(1845/6-1928)
missionary and schoolmistress
Always called Mary, she was the daughter of Pentire Anderson Morshead and Charlotte Story; her paternal grandmother was a Yonge which made her a cousin of CMY. She joined the Gosling Society as ‘Bog Oak’ and was one of the liveliest contributors to The Barnacle, she left when she went to South Africa to do missionary and social work in 1868; she later wrote a history of the Universities Mission to Central Africa 1859-1896 (1897) and  A Pioneer and Founder . . . Robert Gray, first Bishop of Cape Town (1905).  She  also edited Eikon Basilike (1887). For many years, from at least 1881, she was a matron at Marlborough St Giles Industrial School for Girls, Andover. Many of her contributions to MP are signed ‘Bog Oak’. Jackson’s Oxford Journal (30 May 1891) reports her speaking at a GFS conference as a member of the committee for the Winchester Diocese. She was first cousin to fellow-Goslings Mary Sunderland and Anne Baker.

title: Theodora M. Louisa Lane Clarke
text:
(1851-1906)
member of the Gosling Society
A Gosling as ‘Joy’ in 1869, her address was L’Hyvreuse Lodge, Cambridge Park, Guernsey. She was evidently a friend of Mary Augusta Arnold’s. Only child of Rev. Thomas Clarke, Rector of Woodeaton, Oxford, and Louisa Lane, author of topographical and historical works on the Channel Islands, she married (1879) Bartholomew (or Bartle) Teeling (1848-1921), Captain in the Rifle Brigade, Secretary 1872 of the Catholic Union of Ireland, Private Chamberlain to the Pope.  She published journalism, a story, a play, music, sometimes as ‘Norman Stuart’ or ’Isola.  [WWW both husband and wife, Priaulx Library, wills index.]

title: The Hon. James Colborne
text:
2nd Lord Seaton
(8 Sept 1816-11 Oct 1888)
cousin of CMY
He married (12 Feb 1851) the Hon. Charlotte de Burgh.  CMY and her mother were present at his wedding, which was reported in The Times (14 February 1851): 'On the 12th inst., by special licence, at St. Peter's Church, Pimlico, by the Rev. John Yonge, rector of Newton Ferrers, assisted by the Rev. Dr. Harris, incumbent of Tor Mohun, Major the Hon. James Colborne, eldest son of Lieutenant-General Lord Seaton, to the Hon. Charlotte de Burgh, youngest daughter of Lieutenant-General Lord Downes.' [PB 1914; tomb Newton Ferrars church]

title: Nina Cole
text:
correspondent, evidently a friend of the Colborne family.
If the book she sent CMY in 1874 was Nina Cole, Meditations on the Song of Songs (London: Hayes 1874), an anonymous volume identified by Halkett and Laing, that may have been her name. A person of this name was a committee member of the Church and Stage Guild (founded 1879). Its objects, according to an advertisement in The Era (13 July 1879) were 'to promote religious and social sympathy between the members of the Church and of the Stage'.
However, another possible candidate is Anna Cole (1832/3-1909), daughter of the Rev. John Francis Cole, Vicar of Kirdford, near Petworth, Sussex, who was a subscriber to MP and appears in its correspondence column (December 1877) 592.

title: Arthur Duke Coleridge
text:
(1830/1-1913)
father of Mary Elizabeth Coleridge
Third son of Francis Coleridge, solicitor, of Ottery (brother to Sir John Taylor Coleridge) he was the elder brother of Alice Mary Coleridge, and a cousin of Coleridge Patteson and Mary Coleridge. His mother Frances Duke (Taylor) Coleridge was a first cousin of CMY’s grandfather the Rev. Duke Yonge, so they were third cousins. [Obituaries Times 30, 31 October 1913]

title: John Yonge Anderson Morshead
text:
(11 June 1846-1923)
cousin of CMY, barrister
Eldest son of the Rev. John Philip Anderson Morshead and Alethea Yonge, he graduated from University College Oxford in 1869, and qualified as a barrister at the Middle Temple in 1873. He married (21 April 1880) his cousin Helen Beatrice Anderson Morshead (1853/4-1922). He was interested in local history and has an obituary in Transactions of the Devonshire Association 55,56 (1924).

text:
(24 June 1832-24 Jan 1911)

title: Mary Frances Keble Coleridge
text:
(1824-25 March 1898)
daughter of Sir [[person:344]John Taylor Coleridge], cousin and lifelong friend of CMY, member of the Gosling Society
The intimacy between CMY and Mary Coleridge is not well documented because the letters to her were, Christabel Coleridge states, burnt in an accident, but she is very frequently referred to in letters to others. Her 1845 engagement to Roundell Palmer, later 1st Earl of Selborne, was broken off, and she never married. She was devoted to her niece Mildred, daughter of Lord Coleridge, and encouraged CMY to found the Gosling Society partly in order to find an outlet for Mildred's intellectual interests. Her Gosling name was The Old Secretary, but when Mildred lost interest in the society, she did likewise. She was devastated by the furore over Mildred's 1885 marriage to Charles Warren Adams, who had sued Bernard Coleridge for libel. In the same year her brother Lord Coleridge had married Amy Lawford, who had previously lived as the wife of her first cousin, but had then had their Gretna Green marriage declared invalid. It appears that Mary Coleridge, like CMY, consequently disapproved of the second Lady Coleridge, and deplored her influence on her husband. [PB: Coleridge, Story of a Devonshire House]
A portrait of her and her sister Alethea as girls sold in 2006 is illustrated at www.artnet.com/Artists/LotDetailPage.aspx?lot_id=5830456F7F89012700A33CFAA5D

title: George Collins
text:
(b.1818/9)
groom and gardener

After Martha's death he married a younger woman named Emma, who also acted as postmistress of Otterbourne, and had at least one daughter, Frances (b. 1876), who qualified as a teacher and was the mistress of Otterbourne School.

title: Harriet Lucy Cox
text:
(b.1825/6)
A Miss H. L. Cox was the recipient of payments from CMY from 1857.
Harriet Lucy Cox, who later married the Rev. Arthur Baker, was presented with a copy of The Daisy Chain by CMY (10 June 1858), inscribed 'H.L. Cox, from her sincere friend C M Yonge'. Mrs Baker transcribed books into Braille, and it is possible that the payments relate to that, but it seems more likely that she was a contributor. [Information from Ms Delia Twamley, who owns the book.]
The census returns show a Harriet E. L. Cox (b. Waddesdon Bucks. 1825/6), daughter of the Rev. Frederick Cox, Vicar of Upper Winchendon, who married the Rev. Arthur Baker (1817-1868), and is presumed to be this person. Since [[otherbook:8]'Sunlight in the Clouds'] was signed H.L.C. when it appeared in MP it is possible that she was the author of that work and therefore also of '[[otherbook:5]An Object in Life'].

title: Lady Crauford
text:
contributor to Mothers in Council (1797)
CMY’s correspondent has evidently answered a Puzzled Mother in MP in 1896. Of the three answers printed in Mothers in Council 6 (January 1897) the one which most accords with CMY’s comment is signed ‘Isolda’, and the letter was probably addressed to the Hon. Isolda Caroline Vereker (1852/3-1927), daughter of the 4th Viscount Gort and wife of Sir Charles Craufurd 4th Bt. (1847-1939), R.N..

title: Edith Crawley
text:
(b.1837)
missionary, cousin of CMY
She sailed for Cape Town with Bishop Gray and Mary Anderson Morshead, leaving Plymouth on 10 October 1868. She was the daughter of George Abraham Crawley (1795-1862), solicitor, and granddaughter of the Rev. Charles Crawley (1756-1849), Rector of Stowe Nine Churches and brother of CMY's grandmother Catharina (Crawley) Yonge. After her return from South Africa Edith Crawley seems to have lived with her brother, also the Rev. Charles Crawley.
[Morshead, Robert Gray]

title: Captain Charles Francis Cromie
text:
(1858-1907)
nephew-in-law of CMY
He entered the 37th Hampshire Regiment in 1878; he became captain in 1884; went to Staff College 1890-1891; and retired from the army in 1892. He married, first (1881) Mary Grace Webb Bowen, and had at least one son born in 1882. He petitioned for divorce on the grounds of her adultery with Captain Coddington, RN, commander of the ship Duke of Wellington; this was granted 11 May 1892. He married, secondly (1894), CMY’s niece Joanna Angela Yonge. He served as Vice-Consul at Dar-al-Baida, Morocco, 1894-1899; and Algeciras, 1899-1902; and was Consul at Dakar and for the Republic of Liberia, 1902-1905. The Who’s Who entry of his son by his first wife calls her Mrs Lennard; she seems to have remarried John Milner Lennard in 1905. [Daily News (12 May 1892); WWW C.F. Cromie and Francis Cromie (1882-1918]]

title: The Rev. John Philip Anderson Morshead
text:
(c.1809-7 Sept 1881)
cousin of CMY
He matriculated from Exeter Coll 1827 and from 1854 was Vicar of Salcombe Regis. His grandmother was Elizabeth Yonge, sister of the Rev. Duke Yonge, Vicar of Cornwood, so he and his brother Captain Pentyre Anderson Morshead were cousins of the Yonges of Puslinch and of CMY. He married (1845) his second cousin Alethea Yonge (1815-1863), eldest daughter of the Rev. John Yonge of Puslinch.

title: The Rev. Canon James Duncan
text:
(1839-1895)
secretary of the National Society
Ordained (1863) by the Bishop of Winchester, he served as curate of Alverstoke, Hants 1862-67, and Christ Church, Albany Street, London 1867-70. He served as secretary of the National Society for the Education of the Poor in Accordance with the Principles of the Established Church from 1871 till 1891 when he resigned to become a canon of Canterbury. [MS Lambeth Palace 4503 ff.63-5, letter from E.W. Benson; Crockfords; brief obituary Leeds Mercury (12 January 1895); canonry Daily News 26 May 1891.]

title: Mary Anne Dyson
text:
(28 March 1809-29 Sept 1878)
writer and schoolmistress
Life-long friend of CMY, she lived with her much older half-brother, the Rev. Charles Dyson, Rector of Dogmersfield, until his death in 1860, and latterly at Church Crookham. Her will was proved by her nephew the Rev Francis Dyson, estate under £10,000. She was an invalid ‘confined her to a sofa and latterly even to her bed’ ; author of Conversations with Cousin Rachel, Little Alice and her Sister, The White Kitten, Out in the Dark, Ivo and Verena, [wills index, obit MP (1878, 2, 521-5]

title: Lady Caroline Georgiana Eliot
text:
(1799/1800-1866)
headmistress
She was recipient of sums from CMY from 1856, headmistress of a large [for then] girls' school, St. Michael's College, Bognor. This was a school associated with the Woodard schools, and closed, as St. Michael's, Petworth, in 1994. She was eldest daughter of William, 2nd Earl of St. Germans (1767-1845). Her stepmother Susan Mordaunt was the aunt of Mary Anne Dyson's friend Mary (Mordaunt), Lady Acland. [PB; 1861 census] Her successor as headmistress of St. Michael's was Frances Wheeler.

title: Maria Georgina Archer Houblon
text:
(b. 1853)
member of the Gosling Society
Daughter of the Rev. Thomas Archer Houblon (1808-1874) and Eleanor Deedes (d.1865).  A Gosling as Polypodium, she lived in December 1876 at Peasmore Rectory, Newbury.  Her father had been one of W.J. Butler’s curates at Wantage;  her only brother, the Rev.  T. H. Archer Houblon (1849-1933) succeeded him as Vicar of Wantage, 1881-1907, and Maria kept house for him.  She became an Associate of the Wantage sisterhood in 1870.  Of their three sisters Eleanor (d. 1917) became an associate in 1866;  Sophia (1844-1927) became a professed sister of the community in 1878 and Reverend Mother 1912-1920; and Louisa Margaret (d. 1924) became an associate in 1871, professed sister in 1880 and Reverend Mother of the daughter house of the Holy Rood, Worthing.  They were cousins of other Goslings Elisabeth Forbes and Rachel Deedes and were also related to the Bramstons [LG 1952, Lady Anne Archer Houblon, The Houblon Family;  Wantage archives;  WWW;  Journal of Clarissa Trant]

title: Marion Finlaison
text:
(b. London 1834/5-1914)
schoolmistress, subeditor of MP
CMY's neighbour Marion Finlaison (1834/5-1914) is described in Coleridge, [[otherbook:40]Life], 272, 280. She ran a small boarding-school in Otterbourne and acted as sub-editor of MP. Although Marion is what she wrote on the census forms, it appears she was actually baptised Catherine Jane. She was the daughter of William Finlaison (1789-1852), Captain RN (he was brother of the actuary and was on the Beagle, briefly also governor of Ascension I) and Mary Anne Ovenden. In 1861, calling herself Marion C. C [or J?]. Finlaison, she was living with her mother in the household of her sister Mary Isobell (1819-1900) and brother-in-law, the Rev. [[person:758]Henry Jenner] (1820-1898), Rector of Preston, Kent. Jenner was later made Bishop of Dunedin New Zealand (though rejected as too High) and Bishop of the Eglise Catholique Gallicane. Marion Finlaison acted as subeditor of the MP and ran a small boarding school in Otterbourne from late 1870s, probably successor of the school run by Mary Adams. She was evidently known by her family as Kate. She lived at Wingham between Preston and Otterbourne. [Not in PO Directory 1875, listed in White's Directory 1878; Finlaison letters; geocities.]

Jenner was later made Bishop of Dunedin New Zealand (though rejected as too High) and Bishop of the Eglise Catholique Gallicane. Marion Finlaison acted as subeditor of the MP and ran a small boarding school in Otterbourne from late 1870s, probably successor of the school run by Mary Adams. She was evidently known by her family as Kate. She lived at Wingham between Preston and Otterbourne. [Not in PO Directory 1875, listed in White's Directory 1878; Finlaison letters; geocities.]

title: Margaret (Scott) Gatty
text:
(1809-1873)
children’s writer and naturalist
She married (8 July 1839) the Rev. Alfred Gatty (1813-1903), Vicar of Ecclesfield; she was the mother of Juliana Horatia (Gatty) Ewing, and a contributor to MP (1857-69). Gatty’s Aunt Judy’s Tales (1859) and Aunt Judy’s Letters (1862) were made up of contributions to the MP from 1858 to 1865. 'Aunt Judy' was there represented as an older sister telling stories to her younger brothers and sisters, and was based on Gatty’s daughter [[person:494]Juliana Horatia Gatty] (1841-1885), later Ewing, who herself began to contribute to the MP in 1861. In 1866 Gatty began to edit a periodical for George Bell called [[other:54]Aunt Judy’s Magazine] and most of the later fiction of both mother and daughter appeared there.

title: Selina Gaye
text:
(Westminster 1839/40-1914)
contributor to MP (1866-89)
Elder daughter of the Rev. Charles Gaye, Rector of St. Matthews, Ipswich, she was author of fiction and non-fiction for children, including translations from Hungarian, sometimes as S.G. and sometimes as ‘A clergyman’s daughter’. She may well be the author of all contributions to MP signed S.G.. By the 1871 census she was an authoress living in London, Coleshill St., with an elderly ?aunt;  in 1891 and 1901 in London with brother Howard Gaye, architect, with no servant.

title: Henry Hucks Gibbs
text:
1st Lord Aldenham
(1819-1907)
cousin of CMY, philologist
He was created (1896) 1st Lord Aldenham. He married (1845) Louisa Anne Adams (d. 1895). He was son of George Henry Gibbs (1785-1842), elder brother of William Gibbs of Tyntesfield. His parents were first cousins, his mother being Caroline, sixth daughter of the Rev. Charles Crawley (1756-1849), Rector of Stowe Nine Churches, Northants., and Mary Gibbs (d.1819). He was thus CMY’s second cousin. [ODNB, PB 1952]

He was Master of St. Cross Hospital, Rector of St. Mary's, Southampton, and of Old Alresford, New Alresford and Medstead, mentioned by CMY (16 Jan 1854). He was a notorious pluralist and sparked the debates which gave rise to Trollope’s The Warden.

brother-in-law of Gertrude Walter
He married (1866) Louisa, second daughter of Lt- Col Edward Walter. In the 1891 census he was living at Itchen Cottage, Itchen Abbas with his wife and daughters Georgina, 23, Edith, 22,  Dora E., 17, Gertrude, 14, Violet, 13, and son Frank, 11.  In the 1901 census he was living at Upcott, Bishops Hall, Somerset,  with his wife Louisa, 59, and daughters Elizabeth, 26, Gertrude, 24, Violet Amy, 22, and three servants. The probate of his will was obtained by Gertrude Louisa Charlotte Manley wife of Harry Lamont Manley.

title: James John Arnold
text:
(b. Peckham 1834/5)
of Boyatt, surveyor and engineer
He married, first, Sarah (Vine) Arnold (Otterbourne 1821/2-1896). In the 1861 census she is a spinster farmer of 39 with a companion niece Marianna Vine (b.1843/4) and he is her boarder, aged 26. He married, secondly in Chelsea in the fourth quarter of 1896, Ellen Vine. He was keeping a hotel in Colchester with his wife Ellen (b. Hargham, Norfolk 1853/4) in 1901. However, since his second wife seems to be the Ellen (Drane) Vine (b. Hargham, Norfolk 1853/4), widow and milliner, living with her father in London in 1881 and 1891, it looks as if CMY’s suspicions that he had married within the forbidden limits were unfounded. Marriage with a deceased wife’s niece was illegal in England and Wales until the passing of the Marriage (Prohibited Degrees) Relationship Act (1931).

title: The Rev. George Collyer Harris
text:
(Toronto, Canada 1833-4 May 1874)
step cousin of CMY, contributor to MP (1869, 1870)
The only son of the Rev. Joseph H. Harris’s first marriage to Charlotte Collyer. She died, probably in child birth in 1834 in Canada and Joseph remarried (1837) Jane Yonge, sister of Uncle Yonge, who had accompanied her sister Lady Seaton to Canada. George Harris was born in Toronto and educated at Harrow and Exeter College Oxford (BA 1856, MA 1858) He served as curate of Chipping Campden 1857-9, then Tor Mohun 1859-65; he was Vicar of St Luke's, Torquay from 1865 until his death. He died at his parents' house, Sorel, Torquay.  He married (London 1865) Percy [sic] Primrose (b. Quebec 1839/40) and had Edward Percy Harris (b.1866/7-?d.1872),  George Montagu Harris (b.1869) and Mary J Y Harris (b.1871), the latter the biographer of Frances Mary Peard. [Boase supp; 1871, 1901, 1881 census, information from Ian Yonge.]

title: The Rev. Joseph Hemington Harris
text:
(1799/1800-25 June 1881)
Vicar of Tor Mohun and Cockington
He married, first, Charlotte Collyer. She died, probably in child birth in 1834 in Canada and H Joseph married (1837), also in Canada, Jane Yonge, CMY's father's first cousin. His only child, the Rev. George Collyer Harris was born of the first marriage. The Rev.Joseph Harris was educated at St Paul’s School and St John’s College, Cambridge, Fellow of Clare Coll, Cambridge 1824-9, priest 1825, principal of Upper Canada College 1829-38, curate of Cockington 1845-8, of Tor Mohun 1848- his death. Died at Sorel, Torquay, [obituary Guardian  29 June 1881, 913 ]

title: Sophia Jane (Collinson) Harrison
text:
(1826-1891)
wife of Thomas Elliot Harrison
Daughter of the Rev. John Collinson, Rector of Boldon, and Amelia King, she married (6 January 1859), as his second wife, Thomas Elliot Harrison (1808-1888), of Whitburn, Sunderland-on-Wear, the distinguished railway engineer and entrepreneur, see ODNB. They had two sons and four daughters; one of the latter, Katharine Charlotte Harrison, was CMY’s goddaughter. CMY stayed with them at Whitburn and at their house in Paddington in London. See the dedication of Grisly Grisell. Sophia Harrison’s sister Thomasine Septima Collinson married the Rev. Henry de Winton (d. 1895), Archdeacon of Brecknock, and is probably the Mrs de Winton mentioned in the letter to her. However, Julia Cecilia Collinson (1812-1876) who married first (1831) Walter de Winton, ne Wilkins (d.1840), and secondly (1857) William Stretton (d 1868) , whose biography CMY wrote in Women Novelists of Queen Victoria’s Reign, was also a member of this family, another sister of Mrs Harrison. [Sutherland, Longman Companion, under Stretton; LG 1952 de Winton; ODNB T. E. Harrison; PB 1914; Coleridge, Life 31-2; corr. N&Q (1908)]

title: Frances Hester (Carter Smith) Atkinson
text:
(1827-1908)
sister of Ann Carter Smith and contributor to MP
She contributed to MP (1855-7) five papers on ‘Traditions of Norway’, with which she had probably become acquainted while the family lived there. Daughter of the Rev. Richard Carter Smith and sister of Ann Carter Smith,  she was christened St. Olave, Hart Street, London, 13 September 1827, and married (1856), on his being appointed Master of Clare College, the Rev. Dr. Edward Atkinson (1819-1915), and died childless.  He was the longest-serving ever head of a Cambridge College.  In his obituary in  The Times  (3 March 1915) 13d, she is described as a niece of a former fellow and tutor of the college (i.e. William Williamson).

title: Jane Margaret (Winnard) Hooper
text:
(1818-1907)
contributor to MP (1863)
The novel published in MP appeared under her name; she was wife of George Hooper, journalist; she published fiction from 1851, including 'Prince Pertinax' which appeared in MP in 1863 (though according to www.copac.ac.uk it did not appear in volume form until 1883). She called herself ‘authoress’ in the 1871 census. Jane Hooper had a daughter Margaret L. Hooper (b. Middlesex 1854/5), who became an artist and illustrator and a son, Winnard Hooper (1853-1935), city editor of The Times. They were perhaps the characters in the story Little Maggie and her brother: A Sketch for Children (1861). CMY recommended it in ’Hints on Reading MP (March 1862) 335, despite having told Ann Carter Smith she didn't care for it.

title: Edith Sophia Jacob
text:
(1836-1918)
contributor to MP (1867)
Daughter of the Venerable Canon Philip Jacob (1802/3-1884), rector from 1831 of Crawley with Hunton, north-west of Winchester, and canon and (from 1860) Archdeacon of Winchester, and his wife, Anna Sophia (1807?-1857), eldest daughter of the Hon. Gerard Thomas Noel, canon of Winchester. The parents were married in 1832. Edith, according to her brother Edgar ‘was left in February, 1857, by the death of her mother . . . the eldest daughter of seven surviving children.’1 There were four sons and three daughters of the marriage; a son who went into the army; another son, who went into the navy, either one of whom may have been Eustace Wilberforce Jacob (b.?1834-9 July 1871), SPG missionary in South Africa; Isabel Margaret (b. 1840/1); Edgar (1844-1920); and Ernest Henry (1849-1894), MRCS. Their father had been brought up partly in Guernsey, and his father John Jacob (1765-1840) had published a history of the island. The family also had many connections with India. Edith lived at home, but became seriously ill early in 1868; her disease was described as ’congestion of the spine’ and she spent most of the rest of her life in varying degrees of invalidism. During this first attack, according to her brother, she reflected on the contribution which invalids could make to the life of the Church. The result of this was a paper ‘Thoughts on invalid life’ published at the suggestion of CMY and reviewed by her in the Church Quarterly Review. This resulted in the foundation of the ‘Society of Watchers and Workers’, an organisation to which Edith Jacob devoted her energies for the rest of her life, and of whose privately printed journal, the Watchword, she was editor. This periodical ran 1880-1972; the BL has only odd volumes, but there is a complete run in Lambeth Palace Library. The BL’s copies are bound with interesting rolls of members of the society; CMY is listed as a member of the executive committee. The activities of the society, which included support for homebound ‘invalids of the cultured classes’, enabling them to direct their energies in prayer and fundraising for charitable objects, are described in more detail in Kathleen Alice Orr, Letters (1907, 1909), a privately printed memoir of a deceased watcher with a memoir by Edith Jacob. The Watchers were especially keen on missions.

title: The Rev. Eustace Wilberforce Jacob
text:
(b.?1834-9 July 1871)
SPG missionary in South Africa; brother of Edgar, Edith and Gertrude Jacob
He died at his father’s rectory at Crawley. He was described on the 1871 census as ‘curate of Rookloof D. Natal’ and in the death announcement in The Times (12 July 1871) as ‘missionary of the SPG of the diocese of Natal and eldest surviving son of the Archdeacon of Winchester’. At the time of the census his wife Marianne F. Jacob (b. 1824/5) was also resident at the Rectory.

title: The Rt. Rev. Henry Lascelles Jenner
text:
(1820-1898)
Bishop of Dunedin, Vicar of Preston
His appointment to the bishopric of Dunedin, New Zealand, was abortive, but he was active there 1866-71. Later he was Bishop of the Eglise Catholique Gallicane 1882-93. His address was Preston Vicarage, Dover. He married Mary Isabel, eldest daughter of Capt Finlaison RN, sister of Marion Finlaison, sub-editor of MP. He was a contributor to MP (1884), and perhaps also the Bishop contributing to MP in 1878 as 'H. L. J.' He was son of Sir Herbert Jenner-Fust, the judge in the Gorham case. It seems probable that he was ‘Vertumnus I’, whose address was Preston Vicarage, Sandwich, who conducted the ‘Monthly Packet Botanical Society’ between June 1879 and November 1882, and who subsequently took part in the Spider competitions as ‘Vertumnus I’. [WWW]

title: Priscilla Hannah Johnston
text:
(1842-1912)
campaigner for Balkan minorities
Coming from a family with a strong record on anti-slavery campaigning and prison visiting, she ran a school for Christian girls in Sarajevo with Paulina Irby, closed it in 1875 as a result of uprising and succoured refugees on Slavonia border. The letter to her (1876) concerns taking clothes to Herzegovina for Serbian refugees; she returned to England 1885. [Paulina Irby and Johnston’s namesake mother have ODNB entries ;D. Anderson, Miss Irby and Her Friends.]

title: Anna Maria Keary
text:
(3 March 1825-March 1879)
writer, contributor to MP (1865-76)
Sixth child of the Rev. William Keary, Rector of Bilton, perpetual curate of Sailcoates, and rector of Nunnington. Her mother was daughter of Hall Plumer of Bilston Hall, brother of Sir Thomas Hall Plumer. She and her sister Eliza went to live in Addison Road 1854, later in Bedford Gardens. She worked in a children's hospital and was rather deaf. They seem to have been related to the Johns family. IIn 1873 she seems to have taken part in the essay-writing part of the Gosling Society, but if she had a sobriquet it is not known. [Eliza Keary Memoir of Annie Keary;Vital records index for death]

title: Charles Awdry
text:
(1847-1912)
partner in W.H. Smith and Son, newsagents
Son of Sir John Wither Awdry (1795-1878), chief justice of Bombay, brother of Frances Awdry, the Rt. Rev. William Awdry and the Rev. Vere Awdry,he married (1876) CMY’s goddaughter Margaret Helen Moberly. Sir John Awdry’s mother Jane Bigg was the sister of Sir William Heathcote’s mother Elizabeth (Bigg) Heathcote, and he was therefore cousin to Sir William and to the Rev. William Bigg Wither.

title: Frances Awdry
text:
(1845-19 March 1927)
contributor to MP
The author of biographies of Patteson, William Heathcote and Anne Mackenzie, she is  not to be confused with her sister-in-law, (Frances) Emily (Moberly) Awdry. The daughter of Sir John Wither Awdry (1795-1878), chief justice of Bombay, sister of the Rt. Rev. William Awdry, Charles Awdry and the Rev. Vere Awdry, she lived with her mother at Notton. Sir John Awdry’s mother Jane Bigg was the sister of Sir William Heathcote’s mother Elizabeth (Bigg) Heathcote, and he was therefore cousin to Sir William and to the Rev. William Bigg Wither. Two of Frances Awdry's brothers married daughters of the Rt. Rev. Dr George Moberly. 
Photographs of the family appear at http://www.family-forest.co.uk/familytree/albums/awdry/awdry-photo.shtml

title: The Rev. Vere Awdry
text:
(14 October 1854-12 July 1928)
Vicar of Ampfield
He succeeded John Frewen Moor as Vicar of Ampfield. He married first (3 August 1887), Emily Man, secondly (27 October 1891), Mary Louisa Man, and thirdly (14 July 1909) Lucy Louisa Bury.  He was father by his third marriage of the Rev. Wilbert Vere Awdry (1911-1997), the author of Thomas the Tank Engine (1946). Son of Sir John Wither Awdry (1795-1878), chief justice of Bombay, he was brother of Frances Awdry, the Rt. Rev. William Awdry and Charles Awdry. Sir John Awdry’s mother Jane Bigg was the sister of Sir William Heathcote’s mother Elizabeth (Bigg) Heathcote, and he was therefore cousin to Sir William and to the Rev. William Bigg Wither.

title: Ann Catherine (Johns) Lloyd
text:
(Helston 1847/8-1903 Benares)
artist, member of the Gosling Society
Known as Katie, she was an artist and illustrator, daughter of the Rev. [[person:769]Charles Alexander Johns]. She was a Gosling as Kittiwake in 1864-5. She married in 1873 the Rev. John Henry Lloyd, curate of Wormleighton and subsequently Vicar of Fotheringay. He was curate to the Rev. William Harrison, Vicar of Wormleighton, who was married to Mary Kingsley (1852-1931), daughter of Charles Kingsley and a friend of Katie Johns. Both marriages seem to have come to grief. In 1891 she was living apart from her husband and was matron of Annie Besant’s Women’s Home at 193 Bow Road, a kind of club for girls in the East End, with a few resident matchmakers. A.H.Nethercot The First Five Lives of Annie Besant (Chicago, U of C P 1960) 337, calls her ‘Mrs Kitty Lloyd’ and describes her as ‘a devoted Theosophist’. Later her health broke down and she was sent to Ceylon and then to Benares, where she ended her days as the much-loved Matron of the Central Hindu College Boarding House. She is mentioned in her mother’s will (1895), along with her brother Charles, as having brought her to poverty.[ [census returns 1881, 1891; marriage index; clergy lists; Nethercot, First Five Lives of Annie Besant; Theosophical Yearbook 1933; Obituary in The Theosophist, 1903; info from Professor Joy Dixon]

title: The Rev. John Henry Lloyd
text:
(b. Essex 1846/7)
Vicar of Fotheringhay, husband of Katie Johns
He was educated at Chichester Theological College 1869-71, deacon 1871, priest 1872, curate of St. Michael Lichfield 1871-2, of Maulden, Ampthill 1875, of Clifton Biggleswade 1877-8. He married (1873) Ann Catherine Johns. He was curate in charge of Wormleighton 1880-1 and vicar of Fotheringay 1881-1890, but had no cure by the 1891 volume of Crockfords, and was perhaps living apart from his wife. He disappears from the Clergy List c. 1900, reappears 1907, but is more continuous in Crockfords; he lived at 90 Canonbury Avenue until about 1933. [Crockfords, Clergy List, census returns 1871, 81, 91]

title: Sarah Anne (Northwood) Mercier
text:
(Herts. 1842/3-1917)
contributor to MP (1869-84)
Known as Anne Mercier, she married (1868) the Rev. Jerome John Mercier, Rector of Kemerton, Glos.. A brief obituary in The Times (8 Feb 1917) says that ‘she helped to found the Girls’ Friendly Society as well as the Girls’ High School at Tewkesbury’ and that her son succeeded his father as Rector of Kemerton. She was active in the GFS, and was editor at one time of Friendly Leaves (perhaps taking over from Christabel Coleridge in the twentieth century?).
[COPAC]

title: Charlotte Mary Yonge Letters Collection
text:
Transcription policy
Our aim has been to transcribe each letter accurately without attempting to reproduce on the page the appearance of the manuscript. We have therefore standardized the layout. Each letter has been headed by the recipient’s full name at the time of writing, and the source of the text. Additional information, if available, about the manuscript, the paper and envelope has been added in a footnote. The address has been reduced to one line and left-aligned, although it may have been right-aligned or centred and written over three or four lines in the original. Likewise the date has been reduced to one line and missing information added in square brackets. In the body of the letter the use of insertion marks, deleted text and other information about the appearance of the manuscript has been kept to a minimum, and included only when indispensable to the understanding of the letter. Superscript letters have not been used. Double quotation marks have been avoided where possible. It should be noted that in her correspondence Yonge habitually used such spellings as ‘honor’ and ‘favor’, not used now in British English. A space has been routinely added to separate the signature from the body of the letter. No punctuation has been added, except in brackets occasionally to help the sense. However, Yonge’s lavish use of dashes of various sizes and positions is inaccurately reproduced by typography, and we have therefore sometimes interpreted them as commas and stops. Some paragraphing is editorial.

title: Charlotte Mary Yonge Letters Collection
text:
Copyright
The text of Yonge’s unpublished manuscripts is out of copyright, but the compilation, the annotation and the textual, editorial, and introductory matter are the copyright of the editors, Charlotte Mitchell, Ellen Jordan and Helen Schinske , whose work should be acknowledged in any quotation or reproduction of this work.

title: Charlotte Mary Yonge Letters Collection
text:
Acknowledgements
We acknowledge with gratitude the permission we have received from the owners of manuscripts to transcribe and publish them, and the enormous help and many kindnesses we have received from these and other librarians, archivists and private individuals over many years. We apologise if we have overlooked any cases and would like to be informed of any omissions from the following list so we may correct it. Manuscripts from the Plymouth and West Devon Record Office appear by courtesy of Mr Richard Yonge and we thank the archivist, Anne Morgan, and her colleagues. References to Charlotte Yonge’s bank account appear by courtesy of the partners of Hoare’s Bank. We acknowledge gratefully the courtesy of the following people and institutions in allowing us to publish their manuscripts: Mrs Julia Birley; the late Rosemary, Viscountess Chaplin; Madame Catherine Coste and the Fondation Guizot Val-Richer; the Rev. Canon Geoffrey Curtis; Miss Barbara Dennis; Mrs Philip Drazin; Mrs Caroline Fairclough; Miss Sarah Jardine-Willoughby; Mrs Tig Lang; Dr Charlotte Mitchell; Mr John Purvis; Mrs Clare Roels; Mrs Helen Schinske; Miss Mary Shakeshaft; Dr Susan Walton; Miss Miranda Wenaus; Mrs Katharine Wykes-Sneyd; the Society of Antiquaries of London; the Archives Nationales, Paris; the Seymour Public Library, Auburn; Auckland City Library; the Harry Ransom Humanities Research Center, University of Texas, Austin; the University of Birmingham Library; the Oratory, Birmingham; the Birmingham Reference Library; the Bodleian Library, University of Oxford; Brigham Young University Library; the British Library; the John Hay Library, Brown University; the Poetry Collection of the University of Buffalo; Cambridge University Library; the members of the Charlotte Yonge Society; the Cheltenham Ladies’ College; Cheshire Record Office; the Rare Book & Manuscript Library, Columbia University in the City of New York; the Library of Congress; the University of Delaware Library; Essex Record Office; the Trustees of the Exeter Municipal Charities for the material in Devon Record Office; Edinburgh University Library, Special Collections Department; Eton College Library; Ewelme Cottage Museum, Auckland;
